The current method to collect sand flies is by human landing, which involves placing a person in an area where sand flies are present and allowing them to land on their skin.
The use of traps to replace human volunteers is an attractive alternative, but has not been evaluated in field trials.
The ability to identify and characterize these attractants is essential for developing effective control strategies against this disease.
We show that these compounds are highly attractive to the target mosquito species and have potential for use as part of an integrated control strategy against this disease.
This study provides new insights into the olfactory preferences of the major vector of onchocerciasis, and may lead to the development of improved traps for monitoring and control of this disease.
We show that fatty acid synthase ( FASN), an enzyme involved in lipid synthesis, is required for cell survival after exposure to radiation or chemotherapy drugs.
We found that FASN regulates the expression of both transcription factors in different ways.
The incorporation of unnatural amino acids ( uAAs) into proteins has been used as a powerful tool in protein engineering.
Here we show how to use a natural cellular quality control mechanism called the N-end rule pathway to degrade proteins with specific sequences at their amino terminus.

Here, we describe an approach to improve the efficiency of incorporation of nonstandard amino acids by using a proofreading system consisting of a modified tRNA and its cognate aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase.

We have developed an efficient method to incorporate unnatural amino acids into proteins in E. coli, which is based on the use of a new enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of phenylalanine to biphenylalanine.
We also demonstrate that this system can be engineered to incorporate other nonstandard amino acids and thus expand the genetic code.
We found that blocking Hh signaling in the intestine of mice with colitis worsened disease severity and increased mortality.
We show that Hedgehog signaling is required for intestinal homeostasis and that its loss in mice leads to spontaneous colitis and colon cancer.
We found that Hedgehog signaling promotes intestinal homeostasis by increasing the number of regulatory T cells, which are important to prevent inflammation.
Our findings provide insight into the role of the stroma in regulating intestinal homeostasis, and may lead to new therapeutic strategies for treating inflammatory bowel disease.
The UK's housing stock is old and inefficient; improving its energy performance could save money on fuel bills and reduce carbon emissions.
We found that there is no evidence to support the view that increasing home energy efficiency reduces winter deaths or hospital admissions in older people.

Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 ( SARS-CoV-2), the causative agent of COVID-19, has been detected in several animal species.
We investigated an outbreak of SARS-CoV-2 infection among mink on a farm in Utah, USA.
The virus was detected in multiple tissues of infected animals, including lung, spleen, liver, kidney, heart, brain, and blood.
We found that the virus was genetically distinct from previously reported mink isolates and had a mutation in the spike protein that may affect its ability to bind human ACE2 receptors.
Infectious pancreatic necrosis virus ( IPN) is an important fish pathogen in aquaculture worldwide, causing significant economic losses to the industry.
Here we report the crystal structure of the viral protein VP1 from infectious pancreatic necrosis virus ( IPN), which is a member of the Birnaviridae family.
We have determined the structure of the enzyme in complex with its substrate, and we show how it binds to the substrate and how it activates the reaction.
We also show that the N-terminal amino acid of VP1 is essential for the formation of stable RNA: polymerase complexes.
The removal of organic pollutants from wastewater has been a major concern due to their harmful effects on human health and ecosystems.
We demonstrate here that this process can be used to degrade organic pollutants in water under natural sunlight conditions.
The results provide insight into the mechanism of photochemical reactions in nanomaterials and demonstrate the potential for using these materials as catalysts.
The results presented here provide a new approach to study plasmonic photocatalysis and open up opportunities for developing sustainable technologies to remove organic pollutants from water supplies.

In this paper, we use the Ultimatum Game ( UG), a paradigmatic example of social dilemma, to test whether the brain's reward system is sensitive to the fairness of others' actions and decisions.
Here, we use fMRI to examine the brain regions involved in this process and show that they are located in the prefrontal cortex ( PFC).

We show that the brain regions involved in social cognition are not only sensitive to fairness but also to the degree of unfairness, and that these responses are dissociable from feelings of unfairness and from insula activation.

We show that this process is widespread across Arctic glaciers and may be an important component of global carbon cycling.

We found that rabies virus is present in the brain tissue of two species of vampire bats, Desmodus rotundus and Diphylla ecaudata.
We found RABV in the blood, urine, feces, saliva, oral swabs, lungs and kidneys of the two bats.

We found that the two rabies virus strains isolated from vampire bats in Honduras are genetically similar to those circulating among vampire bats in other countries in Central America, suggesting that there is a common origin for these viruses.
The brain is constantly bombarded with sensory information from our environment, but only some of this information is relevant to our current goals and actions.
The brain is able to detect such changes in the environment with high reliability even when it is engaged in other tasks.

We show that attention does not affect the gain of the sensory response but rather the steepness of the response.
We show that attention improves performance by enhancing the neural representation of changes, which is consistent with a model based on basic neuronal circuits.

The ability of plants to sense and respond to pathogen attack is critical for their survival, but this process must be tightly regulated to prevent excessive activation of defense responses that would lead to reduced plant fitness.

We show that the immune receptor SRFR1 negatively regulates the expression of the transcription factor SNC1 and that loss-of-function mutations in SRFR1 cause increased SNC1 expression, leading to autoimmune-like phenotypes.
We found that TPR2 is required for resistance to the immune elicitor flg22 but not to the immune elicitor chitin.
This study reveals novel roles of TOPLESS proteins in plant immunity and sheds light on the complex regulatory network of immune responses in plants.

We found that almost half of the forest loss occurred on large-scale industrial plantations, but that smallholder agriculture also increased its contribution to deforestation.

Our results suggest that demography is an important factor in shaping cultural evolution, and that it may be possible to infer demographic history from cultural data alone.

We describe here a method to produce homogeneous glycoproteins by modifying the carbohydrate structure of the antibody constant region ( Fc) using a chemoenzymatic approach.

The results presented here provide insights into the structural organization of proteins and will be useful for future studies on protein folding and de novo protein design.
Exosomes are extracellular vesicles that carry proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids from one cell to another.

We found that LMP1-containing exosomes induce changes in the expression of genes involved in cell adhesion and migration, as well as in the production of proteolytic enzymes that degrade the ECM.

These findings have important implications for understanding how intestinal immune responses are regulated during infection with parasitic worms, and may lead to the development of new treatments for worm infections.

Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma ( FL-HCC) is a rare subtype of liver cancer with poor prognosis, which has been linked to a specific chromosomal translocation leading to the formation of a chimeric protein composed of the co-chaperone DNAJB1 and the cAMP-dependent protein kinase A catalytic subunit alpha ( PRKACA).

We have developed an animal model of human hepatocellular carcinoma ( HCC) by expressing the fusion protein in mouse livers using CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing and transposon-mediated somatic gene transfer.
We found that the fusion protein is more potent than its normal counterpart in promoting cell proliferation and survival, suggesting that the fusion may have acquired new functions.
This work provides a new therapeutic strategy for treating FL-HCC by targeting the DNAJB1-PRKACA fusion protein.
We have identified and characterized an arachidonate 12-lipoxygenase ( ALOX15) in mice, rats, and pigs.
We show that the expression of these enzymes is conserved in humans and chimpanzees but not in other primates.
The bacterium Helicobacter pylori colonises the stomach and causes chronic gastritis, peptic ulcers and gastric cancer in humans.
H. pylori has only two DNA polymerases, Pol I and Pol III, which are essential for survival in vivo.
DNA polymerase I is an essential enzyme for bacterial growth, but its function has been difficult to study because of its large size and complex structure.
Here we report that yeast RNA polymerase I ( Pol I) is involved in genome stability by preventing the formation of double-strand breaks at the ribosomal DNA ( rDNA).
The duplication of DNA is essential for all living organisms to survive and propagate.
The regulation of DNA replication is essential for maintaining genome integrity, as uncontrolled DNA synthesis leads to genomic instability.
We show that, although most origins are dependent on the MCM helicase for initiation, some origins are independent of MCM.
We found that, when cells were treated with an inhibitor of DNA synthesis, the majority of origins of replication were activated at random, but some were activated preferentially.
Rift Valley fever ( RVF) is an arthropod-borne viral disease of livestock and wildlife, which has been reported in many countries around the world.
Rift Valley fever ( RVF) is an arthropod-borne viral disease of livestock, which has been reported to have caused severe epidemics in Africa and the Arabian Peninsula.

Key results: The review found that kangaroo mother care reduced the risk of death by 40%, sepsis/nosocomial infection by 65%, and hypothermia by 72%.

We wanted to find out whether there is an increased risk of dying, having a heart attack or developing pneumonia after surgery when regional anaesthetic techniques are used instead of general anaesthesia.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant studies published up to July 2012 and found 11 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 10, 000 participants.
Study characteristics We searched the literature up to 5 May 2016 and found 13 studies with over 10,000 participants.
We found no studies that compared the effects of different types of exercise programs for people with PD.
Neuraxial blockade was associated with fewer deaths at 30 days' follow-up compared with general anaesthesia.
The combined results showed that neuraxial blockade reduced the risk of pneumonia by 55%.
The review found no significant differences in the risk of heart attack or stroke between the two types of surgery.
We found that regional anaesthetic techniques may reduce the risk of pneumonia in people undergoing abdominal surgery.
The lens epithelium-derived growth factor ( LEDGF) protein is an essential co-factor for HIV-1 integration, which binds to the integrase protein and helps it to target the virus to the host DNA.

We found that in the absence of LEDGF/p75, HIV-1 infection is severely impaired and that this defect is not due to reduced viral entry or reverse transcription but rather to an inability to establish stable integration of proviruses.
The study of scientific influence has been a long-standing concern in science studies, but it remains unclear how to measure influence across disciplines.
We present a method for analyzing large-scale datasets of scientific articles to identify topics and authors who are likely to be influential in shaping future research.
We find that citation networks reflect both the persistence of individual scientists' contributions and the diversity of their research interests.

Our approach provides a way to measure the diversity of research contributions in terms of their distance from the mainstream literature, which is important for understanding how science works and for evaluating the value of research.
We have discovered a new mechanism for methane hydrate formation in which the initial step is the insertion of water molecules into the interstitial space between two methane molecules.
We show that the hydration layer structure and dynamics play a key role in determining the stability of methane hydrates.
Glioblastoma ( GB) is one of the most aggressive brain tumors with poor prognosis, which has been attributed to its high invasiveness.
We found that NKCC1 is expressed at high levels in gliomas and that its inhibition by bumetanide significantly reduced glioma cell migration and invasion.
Our results provide new insights into how gliomas migrate in vivo and may lead to novel therapeutic strategies for treating this disease.
Our findings suggest that targeting NKCC1 or WNKs could represent a novel strategy to treat GB and potentially other cancers with high metastatic potential.
The bacterial enzyme reverse gyrase is unique among topoisomerases because it introduces positive supercoiling into double-stranded DNA.
However, the mechanism of this process is unknown.
We show that reverse gyrase is able to wind DNA by more than 100 turns in a single reaction, and we use this property to measure the mechanical properties of the enzyme.
We propose that this enzyme is essential for maintaining the stability of the genome under extreme conditions and that it may be an important factor in the evolution of life at high temperatures.
RNA-DNA hybrids, or R-loops, are formed when nascent RNA transcripts fold back and bind to their own template DNA strand.
We show that the probability of forming an R-loop depends on the local sequence context, with some sequences being more prone to form R-loops than others.
We have developed a theoretical framework to study the thermodynamic properties of R-loops and their role in genome stability.

We show that R-loops are not only important for transcription termination but also play an essential role in maintaining genome stability by relaxing the torsional stress generated during transcription.

Here we show that activation of Nrf2, a transcription factor known to protect neurons from oxidative stress and inflammation, reduces the accumulation of misfolded proteins in the brain of a mouse model of Parkinson's disease.
We found that Nrf2 protects against protein aggregation in a cell-autonomous manner and that this effect is time-dependent, with early activation of Nrf2 being more protective than late activation.
We found that Nrf2 decreases a-synuclein protein levels by promoting its degradation, and this is mediated through the proteasome.
We found that Nrf2 is able to rescue the toxicity of diffuse LRRK2 by promoting its aggregation into more benign forms.
Herpesviruses are enveloped viruses with a unique membrane protein complex ( gB/gH/gL) that mediates virus entry into cells.

We have previously shown that the ectodomain of HSV-1 glycoprotein B ( gB) is sufficient to mediate cell-cell fusion and that this activity requires two specific regions within the first domain of gB ( DI).
Here we show that the fusion protein of rabies virus ( RABV) interacts with the host cell membrane and induces membrane curvature through its transmembrane domain ( TMD).

We have identified a single amino acid residue in the glycoprotein B ( gB) envelope protein of varicella zoster virus ( VZV) that is critical for binding to a human antibody that blocks infection by this virus.
We found that the binding site of one of these antibodies ( mAb SG2) is located on the same region as another antibody ( mAb 93k), which we previously showed to be important for blocking HSV-1 entry.
We found that this mutation is essential for efficient virus replication and that it affects the interaction of the viral glycoprotein gE with its cellular receptor nectin-1.
The infectious agent is made up of abnormally folded proteins called prions.
Prions are infectious agents composed of misfolded proteins, and their propagation is based on templated conversion of normal protein molecules into the disease-causing conformations.
We have previously shown that different prion strains display distinct quaternary structures, which are correlated with their respective incubation times in vivo.
We show that the observed behavior is due to the interplay between the size and density of the particles.
This work shows that these small assemblies are highly stable to proteolytic degradation and have high templating activity in vitro.
The mitral cell population in the olfactory bulb exhibits synchronized oscillations at frequencies between 30 and 80 Hz, which are thought to play an essential role in odor perception.
It is thought that this synchronization may be important for information processing in olfactory systems.
However, most studies of synchronization have assumed that the coupling between neurons is fixed in time.
We find that this is possible if the mitral cells are synchronized with each other, which would be consistent with experimental observations.
We find that common inputs from granule cells increase the correlation between mitral cells, which is important for odor coding.

The establishment of pluripotency during early embryonic development requires the removal of epigenetic marks from the genome.
We found that the histone methyltransferase MLL2 is required for proper gene expression in the developing Drosophila ovary.

Our findings suggest that MLL2 plays an important role in the regulation of gene expression during early embryogenesis, possibly by maintaining the proper chromatin structure necessary for the activation of genes involved in early development.

Our results suggest that the function of MLL2 in early embryonic development may be limited to the initial establishment of H3K4 methylation at gene promoters, with other H3K4 methyltransferases taking over as the embryo progresses through gastrulation.
Transcription of DNA by RNA polymerase ( RNAP) generates mechanical stress on the double helix.
However, when the torsion is too high, it can cause RNA polymerase ( RNAP) stalling or backtracking, which can lead to transcriptional arrest.

We used this approach to identify genes that are expressed in the same tissues at similar levels in both young and old mice, and we found that these genes were enriched for those involved in lipid metabolism.
We find that the decline in gene expression is associated with changes in chromatin structure, but not necessarily with changes in the binding of specific transcription factors.
Mechanosensitive channels are proteins that open in response to mechanical stress, such as sudden changes in pressure or tension.
The mechanistic details of this process are not well understood, however.
We report here the crystal structure of an archaeal mechanosensitive channel ( MscS) from Methanosarcina acetivorans in two distinct conformational states.
Our results show how the different parts of the protein move in concert to open the channel, and they reveal the molecular details of the mechanism by which the channel opens.
The movement of cells on surfaces, such as epithelial cells and neurons, is often driven by internal forces generated by molecular motors.
Cells move by extending membrane protrusions, which are highly dynamic structures that extend from the cell body and propel it forward.
We have studied the dynamics of pseudopod extension in Dictyostelium cells using high-speed microscopy and computer simulations.
We show that the formation of pseudopodia is highly ordered and that it is driven by the interaction between the actin cytoskeleton and the plasma membrane.

We have developed a new method to analyze the directionality of cell migration, which is based on the analysis of the distribution of the angle between the direction of motion and the direction of protrusion.
We find that the key parameters are the fraction of persistent pseudopodia, the Left-Right alternation, and the distribution of pseudopod angles.

We show that the centrosome is inherited from the mother worm and that it produces a microtubule array that is essential for sperm activation and egg activation.
We show that Wolbachia are transported along microtubules in the cell, and that this transport is essential for their localization to the posterior of the egg.

We also show that Wolbachia depletion causes defects in the establishment of anterior-posterior polarity and that this is due to the loss of the Wolbachia-induced expression of the Drosophila gene dachshund ( dachs).
This study provides an example of how host and symbionts have evolved together to create a new trait, which is essential for the development of the insect body plan.
The World Health Organization estimates that one-third of all new cases of tuberculosis ( TB) occur among people with HIV infection, but little is known about how to prevent this.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant studies published up until February 2017.
We found no studies in which children were given isoniazid for prevention of tuberculosis (TB) compared to a control group receiving a different drug or no intervention.

The review authors searched for studies in which children under the age of 13 were randomised to receive either vitamin A or placebo (a 'dummy' treatment).
The studies were not designed to measure whether or not the children had TB disease; they only measured whether or not they had evidence of infection.
The origin of mitochondria is one of the most important evolutionary events in eukaryotic cells.
Proteins destined for mitochondria are imported via the translocase of the outer membrane ( TOM) complex.
Here, we show that TvTom40-2 is an essential component of the mitochondrial outer membrane and functions as a translocon for importing proteins from the cytosol into the mitochondrion.
We also show that TvTOM interacts with SAM50, which is required for the biogenesis of b-barrels.
Breast cancer is one of the most common cancers in women, and it has been estimated that over 1 million new cases will be diagnosed annually by 2015.
We have identified a subpopulation of luminal progenitors in the normal human breast that are enriched for the cell cycle inhibitor p27 and are responsive to estrogen.
We found that the number of cells expressing markers of hormone responsiveness was reduced in the absence of E-cadherin.
We found that p27 binds to thousands of genes in mammary epithelium, including those involved in cell cycle control, growth factor signaling pathways, and transcriptional regulation.
These findings suggest that p27 may play an important role in controlling the number of cells available to become malignant and thus influence breast cancer risk.

Here, we use a combination of experimental and computational approaches to study how mutations in a simple protein affect its function and evolution.

We present a method to correct predictions from arbitrary machine learning algorithms by combining them with a second model trained on data from the same study.
We demonstrate the wide applicability of our method to different types of data and problems.
Genome-wide association studies have identified many disease-associated variants, but most are located in non-coding regions and do not directly affect gene expression levels.
However, these methods do not consider the biological context of the gene expression data.
However, many existing approaches to this problem are not robust to noise in the data or have poor performance when applied to large networks.
We compared six different network-based methods for identifying genetic variants associated with disease risk in a large cohort of families affected by breast cancer.
We found that this solution is enriched for genes involved in cell proliferation and survival.
We provide implementations for all six methods in Python and make them freely available on GitHub.

Bacteria are able to sense and respond to environmental cues, such as light, temperature, or chemicals, by changing gene expression patterns.
However, the metabolic potential of individual bacteria is limited, and they often need to cooperate with other microbes to achieve their ecological functions.
The ability to predict how microbes will respond to new environments is essential for the design of bioreactors and synthetic biology circuits.
We developed a computational framework for studying the metabolic interactions between two bacterial species in a dynamic environment.
In this work, we use genome-scale metabolic models to predict the production of novel compounds by microbial communities.
Our work demonstrates the power of synthetic biology for engineering microbial consortia with new functions.
The relationship between telomere length and age has been well established, but its clinical application remains unclear.
We found that TL was highly variable in healthy individuals, but had a consistent pattern of variation across different tissues.

We found that the length of telomeres in blood cells from patients with the short telomere syndrome was significantly shorter than those from healthy individuals, and that the degree of telomere shortening increased with age.
In this study we show that the telomere length of hematopoietic stem cells is regulated by the transcription factor E2F1, which is activated by p53.
Our results suggest that flow cytometry-based telomere length analysis may be useful in selected patients with CLL or MDS.

Exposure to ambient air pollution has been linked to increased risk of respiratory disease in children; however, few studies have examined the effects of ultrafine particles ( UFPs), which are smaller than PM2.
However, the mechanisms by which UFPs affect asthma remain poorly understood.

These findings suggest that maternal exposure to air pollution during pregnancy may alter the developing lung's ability to respond to common allergens and increase susceptibility to respiratory infections.
However, it is unclear how infants and young children are able to clear carriage in the absence of protective antibodies.
Intranasal immunization of mice with live Streptococcus pneumoniae ( S. pneumoniae) induces protective immunity to pneumococcal carriage in a CD4+ T cell-dependent manner.

We found that in mice with chronic colonization, the immune response to S. pneumoniae is not only protective against infection but also promotes clearance of bacteria from the upper respiratory tract.
We found that adoptively transferred CD4+ T cells were able to protect against infection in immunodeficient mice, and that this protection required both IFN-g and IL-17A/F.
Our results suggest that IL-17A is an important component of protective immunity to pneumococcus, and that it may be useful as a vaccine target.

We present a method, called Local Conformal Autoencoder ( LOCA), which is able to learn a mapping from high-dimensional data to low-dimensional representations in a way that preserves the local geometry of the original space.
LOCA has several advantages over existing methods for dimensionality reduction and clustering analysis, including robustness to noise, high accuracy, and fast computation speed.
Mesenchymal stem/stromal cells ( MSCs) are widely used for regenerative medicine due to their ability to modulate the immune system and promote tissue repair.

We found that MSCs promoted tolerance to allogeneic grafts through the induction of tolerogenic macrophages that express high levels of immunosuppressive cytokines such as IL-10 and are capable of suppressing effector T cell responses.
These findings provide new insights into the mechanisms by which MSCs exert their immunomodulatory effects and may lead to novel strategies for treating autoimmune diseases.

Our results provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms of ZIKV pathogenesis and highlight the importance of E-V473M in enhancing ZIKV virulence and transmissibility.
Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi ( S. Typhi) is the causative agent of typhoid fever, while S. Paratyphi A causes paratyphoid fever.
The aim of this study was to evaluate the performance of a new, electricity-free incubator for newborn care in low-resource settings.
In this study, we evaluated the performance of two different methods to detect typhoid fever in children with fever and diarrhea.
The results of this study suggest that the use of these tests may provide an alternative method for diagnosing typhoid fever in resource-poor areas where laboratory facilities are not available.
Inflammation plays an important role in the pathogenesis of severe malaria.
The mechanisms by which this occurs are not well understood.

The Terminal Classic period ( AD 750-900) is characterized by widespread abandonment and decline of Classic Maya cities, but the causes are unknown.

Abdominal aortic aneurysms are bulges in the main blood vessel supplying blood to the lower part of the body.
The main treatment options are surgery and endovascular repair (where a stent is placed in the artery to block blood flow to the aneurysm).

The aim of this review was to compare the benefits and harms of immediate surgery versus surveillance (regular check-ups) for people who have an AAA between 4.0 cm and 5.5 cm in diameter, but do not have symptoms.

We wanted to know whether or not it is better for patients with an AAA of 4.0-5.5 cm (the size at which they are usually recommended for surgery) to have an operation immediately or to be monitored by ultrasound scan every 6 months until their AAA reaches 5.5 cm.
We found one trial (the Aneurysm Detection and Treatment Study) that compared aneurysm surgery with regular ultrasound scans for monitoring.
Study characteristics We found four studies with a total of 3314 participants.
The review found four randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of abdominal aortic aneurysm screening, which included 11,000 participants.
Key results The four studies found that there was little or no difference between the two groups in terms of overall survival at one year and beyond.

We found that, when we combined results from two studies with long-term follow-up, there was no evidence that patients who had an immediate operation were more likely to survive than those who were monitored for a period of time before having surgery.
However, there was no evidence of an increased risk of death or stroke in those who had surgery.
Smoking cessation interventions for people with severe mental illness Review question We wanted to find out if there are effective ways to help people with severe mental illness to quit smoking.
We found no evidence for the effectiveness of any particular treatment or combination of treatments, but we did find some promising approaches that require further evaluation.
We recruited 1000 smokers with severe mental health problems and randomly allocated them to either receive BSC or usual care ( control group).
The review found no evidence of any difference in the risk of cardiovascular disease, stroke, or death from all causes between those who quit and those who did not quit.
We found that, compared with usual general practitioner ( GP) care, the BSC service did not improve quality of life or reduce depression in people with COPD.
We found that, although more people who received the BSC stopped smoking than those who had usual care alone, this difference was not statistically significant.
We found no evidence to suggest that either programme helped people to quit smoking at 3 or 6 months.
The study did not find any evidence that the programme reduced the number of people who quit smoking or reduced their use of other drugs.
We found that it is possible to recruit people with severe mental illness to take part in a study, but we also found that they were not as interested in quitting as we had hoped.
The results from this study will be used to inform a larger trial, which will look at whether or not the programme is effective in helping people with COPD to quit smoking.

Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) for treating chronic pain in adults Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials on the use of transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) for treating chronic pain in adults.
TENS is thought to work by stimulating nerve fibres in the skin and muscles which carry pain signals to the brain.
We found some evidence that TENS is more effective than placebo at reducing pain, but this was not consistent across all studies.
Quality of evidence The quality of the evidence was low to very low for all outcomes due to small numbers of participants, poor reporting of study methods and some indication of bias.

Inhaled antibiotics for long-term treatment of cystic fibrosis Review question We reviewed the evidence from studies evaluating the effect of long-term inhaled antibiotics on lung function, nutritional status, quality of life and mortality in people with cystic fibrosis.
Study characteristics We found 18 studies with 3042 children and adults who had received either an intervention or usual care for their ADHD symptoms.
The studies were very different in terms of their design, participants, interventions, outcomes measured and duration of follow-up.
We found 11 studies with 1130 people who received either an inhaled antibiotic or no medication, or usual treatment.
Key results We found some evidence that inhaled antibiotics may improve lung function and reduce exacerbations.
We found no evidence about whether or not this treatment improved overall health, reduced the risk of dying, or had any effect on quality of life.
The review found that there is not enough evidence to support the use of antibiotics for this condition, and that further research is needed.
The most important side effects of the treatment were tinnitus (ringing in the ears) and voice alteration, which occurred more often with inhaled antibiotics than with placebo.

Japanese Encephalitis ( JE) is a mosquito-borne viral infection transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes.

We found that SMCL-1 is required for proper chromosome segregation in meiosis I and II, and that its absence causes defects in condensin localization to chromosomes.
We find that SMCL-1 is required for proper chromosome segregation during meiosis and mitosis, and that it interacts with the condensin I and II complexes.
This study highlights the importance of the evolution of protein function, and provides insight into the molecular mechanisms underlying the regulation of chromosome structure and segregation.

The review found that adding hyaluronic acid to culture medium improved the chances of live birth and clinical pregnancy for women undergoing IVF and ICSI.

The addition of hyaluronic acid to IVF protocols has been shown to improve outcomes, but it is unclear whether or not this effect is due to its role in improving embryo implantation or improving endometrial receptivity.
More research is needed on how well these drugs work and whether they are safe for women undergoing IVF treatment.

Mitochondria are organelles found in eukaryotic cells and play an essential role in energy production, cell signaling, and regulation of apoptosis.

Mitochondrial dysfunction is associated with many human diseases, and identifying the molecular mechanisms underlying these disorders will help us develop new treatments.
Adenosine deamination, which converts adenosine to inosine, is an important post-transcriptional modification found in all eukaryotic tRNAs.
The effect of this modification on translation efficiency and protein production has been studied extensively; however, its impact on mRNA abundance remains unknown.

Nrf2 is a key regulator of antioxidant defenses in cells and has been implicated in many diseases including cancer, diabetes, and neurodegenerative disorders.

In this study, we show that the C. elegans Nrf homolog SKN-1 regulates neurotransmitter release and postsynaptic receptor clustering at the neuromuscular junction ( NMJ) through its regulation of the vesicular glutamate transporter VGLUT-1.
We find that SKN-1 is required for normal neuromuscular function and that its activity is regulated by the mitochondrial unfolded protein response ( UPR^mt).

We find that SKN-1 activity is regulated by the conserved WD repeat protein WDR-23, which acts as a repressor of SKN-1 expression in the intestinal epithelium to maintain normal neuromuscular function.
We also show that loss of WDR-23 leads to increased expression of stress response and neuronal signaling genes.
Our findings suggest that SKN-1/Nrf2 may be an important regulator of neuronal function and survival, and that it may play a role in neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease.

We found that participants' decisions were influenced by the probability of each category in their environment and that they used a simple strategy to update this probability over time.
Here, we develop a new computational model of perceptual decision making that incorporates this information to predict human performance on a visual discrimination task.

We found that participants' behavior was best described by a model in which they use a simple rule for estimating probabilities ( i.e., an exponential average) but also have some degree of uncertainty about their own beliefs.

We found that FoxO6 is expressed in craniofacial tissues, and its loss causes craniofacial defects by regulating Hippo signaling and cell proliferation.
Our study identifies a novel mechanism by which the Hippo signaling pathway regulates craniofacial development in mammals.

The introduction of community treatment orders ( CTOs) in 2008 aimed to reduce hospital admissions for people with severe mental illness who are deemed to be 'unwell' enough to require care but not ill enough to be detained under the Mental Health Act.

We recruited 336 people who had been diagnosed with psychosis and were being treated as outpatients.
We found that CTOs were perceived to be focused on medication and that this was seen as being at odds with their role in providing care.

We reviewed the research output on rabies in India over the last decade ( 2008-2017) and found that it is dominated by studies of dog vaccination, which are not aligned with current national policy priorities.
The results showed that biomedical research was the dominant type of research in terms of number of publications and citations.
This study provides evidence of the need to develop a new research agenda that focuses on rabies control rather than just disease prevention.

Ribosome biogenesis is a highly conserved and essential cellular process that involves the synthesis of two large RNA molecules ( rRNA) and their assembly with proteins to form functional ribosomes.
The assembly of ribosomes is a complex process in which hundreds of factors participate to ensure proper formation of functional ribosomes.
We have systematically analyzed the function of 464 proteins predicted to be involved in ribosome assembly using RNA interference ( RNAi).
We used a novel approach combining high-throughput proteomics and machine learning to identify proteins involved in ribosome biogenesis.
We found that the process is highly conserved in evolution and that it involves several proteins that have been previously implicated in ribosome biogenesis.

Cot nursing for preterm babies Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether or not to use cots (beds) instead of incubators for preterm babies.
The main reasons for using incubators are to keep babies warm and safe from infection.

Cot-nursing compared to incubator care for preterm babies Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials comparing cot-nursing with incubator care for preterm infants (born before 37 weeks' gestation).
The review of trials found no evidence that babies who are cared for in an incubator have better outcomes than those cared for on a standard cot bed.
The review of trials found that there was no clear evidence that either method is better than the other for improving outcomes for preterm infants.
The review authors identified four randomised controlled trials that compared cot-nursery care with incubator care, involving 203 newborns.
The review found that cot-nursing is as effective as incubators in keeping babies at a stable body temperature, but it may be less effective than incubators in maintaining oxygen levels.

The unfolded protein response ( UPR) is a stress response that aims to restore normal protein homeostasis by increasing protein synthesis and degradation.

We found 70 studies that met our inclusion criteria for analysis.

The pyrenoid is a unique, membraneless organelle found in algae and cyanobacteria where CO2 is fixed by Rubisco.

Our results suggest that the EPYC1 protein is involved in the formation of the central core of the pyrenoid, and we present two possible structural models for how this might occur.
Our results provide insights into how pyrenoids are assembled and may be engineered in plants for enhanced photosynthesis.

The potato spindle tuber viroid ( PSTVd) is one such RNA virus that causes severe disease symptoms on potato plants.

We found that the expression of StTCP23 is negatively regulated by PSTVd infection and that this regulation is mediated through an RNA-RNA interaction between StTCP23 and the viral virulence modulator region ( VMR).
We found that StTCP23 is cleaved by virus-derived small RNAs, and this cleavage is required for efficient virus infection in planta.
We found that expression of these small RNAs in transgenic potato plants results in phenotypes similar to those observed in PSTVd-infected plants, including stunted growth and reduced leaf size.
We found that StTCP23 is required for normal tuber formation by regulating gibberellin ( GA) biosynthesis and signaling.

Our study provides new insights into how virus infection affects host gene regulation at the post-transcriptional level and may have implications for understanding the molecular mechanisms underlying potato tuberization.

Identifying the long-term effects of human activities on ecosystems has been challenging because most archaeological sites are located in remote areas with limited access to modern environmental data.

We show that this relationship is robust to multiple tests for confounding factors, including climate, vegetation, and population size.

Dengue is the most common mosquito-borne viral disease in humans and affects over 100 million people each year.
The Wolbachia bacteria are naturally occurring endosymbionts of insects and have been shown to be effective at blocking dengue virus infection in mosquitoes.
Wolbachia bacteria are being used to control dengue fever by infecting mosquitoes with them, which blocks the transmission of the disease.
Here, we show that Wolbachia infection does not affect the ability of Aedes aegypti to transmit dengue virus ( DENV) after feeding on an infected host.

We found that this strain of Wolbachia significantly reduced the transmission of dengue virus by reducing the proportion of infected mosquitoes that survive long enough to transmit the virus to humans.

We found that the reduced saliva production of wMel mosquitoes was due to a reduction in the number of salivary glands, and that this is likely caused by the presence of Wolbachia bacteria within the mosquito cells.
These results provide new insights into how Wolbachia affects mosquito biology, which may be useful for developing novel control strategies to prevent dengue transmission.

In this study we examined the prevalence of Toxoplasma gondii infection in two different populations from Kyrgyzstan, one living in an area with high risk for brucellosis ( rural) and one with low risk ( urban).

We found that 5% of people living in rural areas and 2% of those living in urban areas had antibodies to Toxoplasma gondii, indicating they were infected at some point in their lives.
In this study, we report on the prevalence of Toxoplasma gondii antibodies in humans from the Kyrgyz Republic and provide evidence of a high prevalence of T. gondii infection among pregnant women.

We found that approximately 1 in 5 women of childbearing age in Kyrgyzstan are at risk of acquiring infection with Toxoplasma gondii for the first time during pregnancy, and that this is likely to result in an estimated 173 infants being born each year with congenital toxoplasmosis.
The prevalence of Toxoplasma gondii infection in HIV-infected individuals is high, with an estimated 350-1000 cases per million people living with HIV/AIDS worldwide.
This study provides evidence for a significant burden of disease due to congenital and AIDS-related toxoplasmosis in Kyrgyzstan.
Some studies suggest that bats are potential reservoirs of dengue virus ( DENV), but there is no evidence of natural transmission from bats to humans or other animals.
We found that house-roosting bats are not important for dengue virus ( DENV) transmission, but they may be involved in the maintenance and amplification of the virus within urban areas.
We found no evidence of dengue virus in any mosquito species, but we did find evidence of bats carrying Leptospira.
We found evidence for the presence of dengue virus ( DENV) in wild-caught bats, but we were unable to find any evidence of transmission between bats and humans or other animals.

We found no evidence of dengue virus replication or transmission by mosquitoes from bat blood meals and concluded that bats are not involved in the maintenance of dengue virus circulation in the city.

We have investigated how the host plant responds to infection by different strains of Mesorhizobium loti, which are either able or unable to form nitrogen-fixing nodules on Lotus japonicus.

Here we show that the symbiosis between the legume Lotus japonicus and its symbiotic bacterium Mesorhizobium loti is not only restricted to the root nodules but also involves an endophytic phase in which the bacteria enter the plant via the roots and colonize the stem and leaves.

The INDETERMINATE DOMAINS ( IDD) /BIRD family is a large group of plant-specific transcription factors involved in many aspects of plant growth and development, including leaf morphogenesis, flowering time regulation, and stress responses.
We found that loss of IDD4 function results in increased resistance to bacterial pathogens, suggesting that it acts as an inhibitor of plant defense responses.
We found that IDD4 is phosphorylated by MPK6 in response to flg22, which enhances its DNA binding activity and promotes expression of immune-related genes.
We found that the phosphorylation of IDD4 is required for its function in plant immunity against bacterial pathogens.
We also show that IDD4 is required for normal growth and development in plants.

Study characteristics The evidence is current to May 2015.
The review found that Chinese herbal medicine was more effective than placebo in relieving symptoms of urinary tract infection but not better than antibiotics.

Key results The review found some evidence to suggest that Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) used alone or together with antibiotic treatment may help to clear an acute urinary tract infection, and prevent subsequent infections for up to six months.

Grid cells in the brain encode spatial location by firing at multiple locations within a regular hexagonal pattern.

We show that this system has an optimal performance in terms of information processing and energy consumption.
The grid cell system is thought to be responsible for encoding self-motion and spatial location, but its function has not been fully understood.
The brain has to integrate multiple sources of spatial information, such as visual landmarks or self-motion cues, to build up an internal representation of the environment.

We show that, in contrast to the sparse coding hypothesis, the ability to decode location from neural activity is robust to multiple remappings and that this robustness depends on the spatial structure of the environment.
We show that this is indeed the case and that the output of the grid cell system is highly compressed.

Beta blockers are drugs used to lower blood pressure by reducing heart rate and decreasing the force of each heartbeat.

Protein post-translational modifications ( PTMs) are chemical modifications added to proteins after they have been translated from mRNA.

Our results show that acetylation has a stronger effect on protein-protein interactions than phosphorylation, with acetylation often acting to stabilize protein complexes and phosphorylation destabilize them.
We find that the effect of post-translational modifications ( PTMs) on protein-protein interactions is not simply additive but rather depends on the context in which they occur.

Antibiotics for treating gonorrhoea Gonorrhoea is an infection of the reproductive tract caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae bacteria.
The current treatment for this disease is antibiotics such as ceftriaxone, which are given intravenously or by injection into a muscle.
We wanted to find out if using gentamicin instead of ceftriaxone would be better for patients with gonorrhoea, and whether it would be good value for money.
We compared two antibiotics, gentamicin and ceftriaxone, in terms of their effectiveness at treating acute bacterial conjunctivitis in children aged under 5 years.
We found that children who had been treated with antibiotics were less likely to have a severe case of dengue fever, and they recovered faster than those who did not receive antibiotics.
The results showed that there was no significant difference in cure rates between patients treated with either antibiotic, but that the cure rate was lower than expected.
The cure rate for gonorrhoea was high with both treatments, but there were more failures in the gentamicin group than in the ceftriaxone group.
The main side effects were reported to be pain and redness around the injection site, with no significant difference between the antibiotics in terms of other adverse events.
We also looked at how much it would cost to treat people with gentamicin and found that it was more expensive than standard care.
The antibiotics gentamicin and ceftriaxone were found to be safe in this study.

In bacteria, sigma factors are proteins that bind to RNA polymerase and direct it to specific genes for transcription initiation.
In bacteria, different sigma factors compete with each other for access to the RNA polymerase ( RNAP), which is the enzyme responsible for transcribing DNA into RNA.
We developed a mathematical model that accounts for the interactions between RNA polymerase and different sigma factors as well as their effects on transcription initiation and termination.
We validate our model using experimental data and show that it accurately predicts the outcome of competition between different strains.

We also show that the increase in core RNAP availability is not sufficient to explain the observed decrease in rRNA synthesis rate and that the reduction in rRNA synthesis is due to reduced initiation frequency.

We show that this phenomenon is due to the competition between different sigma factors for RNA polymerase and that it can be exploited by bacteria to sense environmental changes in nutrient availability.
The identification of host factors essential for virus replication is an important step in understanding how viruses interact with their hosts and identifying potential targets for drug development.
Influenza viruses are enveloped viruses that use their surface glycoprotein, hemagglutinin ( HA), to bind and enter cells.
We found that mice lacking TMPRSS2 were more resistant to infection with human influenza viruses than those expressing normal levels of TMPRSS2.
These findings provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying the pathogenicity of influenza virus infection in humans.
In this study, we show that the Rv1860 protein expressed from an attenuated Mtb strain is highly immunogenic in mice and guinea pigs.
We found that Rv1860 is a secreted protein of Mtb and BCG and that it has immunomodulatory activity in vitro and in vivo.

We found that BCG-TB1860 is more effective than BCG-GFP in eliciting protective immunity against TB infection in mice.
This study provides new insights into how Mtb glycoproteins may interfere with host immunity by targeting dendritic cells.
The study of river meanders has been a long-standing problem in fluid mechanics and geophysics, with many applications to natural systems such as rivers and estuaries.
We show that the orientation of sedimentary structures within bends depends on both the degree of curvature ( sinuosity) and the direction of flow.
We show how these features can be used to infer the direction of flow in the brain, and we demonstrate this method on data from the visual cortex.
Enteroviruses are a large family of viruses that include poliovirus, rhinovirus, coxsackie B virus, echovirus, and enterovirus 71 ( EV71).

We have used this approach to identify key structural features of these antibodies that are important for their ability to bind to enteroviruses, as well as those that contribute to their broad-spectrum activity.
Parent-child interaction therapy (PCIT) is a psychological treatment for young children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD).
We found 10 studies that compared family therapy with other treatments or no treatment.
The studies we found were all randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which are considered to be the best type of study for evaluating treatments.
The majority of studies included people from racial and ethnic groups who are underrepresented in research.
The majority of studies were funded by government agencies, and most were conducted in the United States.
We found no evidence that FAST was associated with any improvements in child or parent functioning.

We used mathematical modeling to estimate the potential impact of increasing pneumococcal conjugate vaccination coverage in China, and found that increasing coverage would reduce invasive pneumococcal disease incidence by 80% and mortality by 90%.
Our results show that the introduction of PCV in the UK has had a major impact on carriage rates of S pneumoniae, with a reduction of over 80% since 2000.
Inferring gene regulatory networks from high-throughput data is an important task in systems biology and has been extensively studied in recent years.

However, existing methods are limited in their ability to infer dynamic gene regulatory networks ( GRNs) from time-course expression profiles due to the high noise and low sample size of clinical samples.
We propose a probabilistic model to infer gene regulatory networks ( GRNs) from single-cell RNA sequencing ( scRNA-seq) data by modeling the temporal dynamics of gene expression in cancer cells.
We prove that our method is robust to noise in the measurements, and we demonstrate its performance on synthetic and real datasets.
We apply our approach to bladder cancer and breast cancer, demonstrating its ability to identify genes with strong association with disease progression and survival.
We also show that the predicted targets of FOXM1 are enriched for genes involved in cell proliferation and survival, and that they are predictive of poor prognosis in breast cancer patients.
This work provides a novel approach to understanding cancer progression and may be used in future studies to identify potential therapeutic targets.
Leprosy is a disease caused by Mycobacterium leprae bacteria that affects nerve cells in the skin and peripheral nerves of the body.
The treatment for leprosy is multi-drug therapy ( MDT) with dapsone, rifampicin and clofazimine.
We found that HLA-B*13: 01 was associated with severe dengue disease ( DHS) in the Indonesian population and validated it as a potential biomarker for DHS in the Papua region.
We conducted a cross-sectional study to investigate the association between diabetes mellitus and leprosy.
We found that HLA-B*13: 01 is the major risk factor for developing DHF in this population.
Sooty mangabey ( SM) are natural hosts for SIVsmmPBj, which replicates at high levels but does not cause disease.
Here, we describe a novel CCR5 variant with a two-base pair deletion ( D2) that encodes a protein that cannot be detected on the cell surface or function as an HIV receptor.
We found that this mutation is present in both human and mouse, and that it is associated with reduced expression of the gene encoding the protein.
We found that the absence of CCR5 did not affect the susceptibility of rhesus macaques to SIVsmm infection.
We found that SIVsmm strains could infect both wild-type and CCR5-deficient human T-cells in vitro, and that they use multiple chemokine receptors for entry.
Our results demonstrate that SIVsmm uses alternative entry pathways in SM and that these pathways are likely to be important for virus replication in vivo.
This study provides evidence for an alternative route of SIVsmm infection, which may allow the virus to replicate efficiently in the absence of CCR5 expression on CD4 T lymphocytes.
The role of Tregs in COVID-19 is unknown.
We found that severe COVID-19 patients have increased numbers of regulatory T cells ( Tregs) and high levels of the Treg-specific transcription factor FoxP3.
We found that most of these changes were present at the time of hospitalization and persisted for several months after recovery from acute illness.
Our study suggests that Tregs may have a dual role in COVID-19, as they are both protective against excessive inflammation but also suppress anti-SARS-CoV-2 immune responses.

We show that the model is able to reproduce experimental data from different studies and that it has two stable states, one of which corresponds to normal brain activity and the other to epilepsy.
The physiological state is maintained by the balance between excitability and refractoriness, which are regulated by ion channel activity.

Our results suggest that the pore structure is an important determinant of drug sensitivity, and that the pore-lining residues may be targeted for future drug development.

The identification and functional characterization of olfactory receptors is challenging because they are G protein-coupled receptors ( GPCRs) that require specific lipid rafts to be expressed properly on the cell surface.

The studies were all conducted in an outpatient setting and included people who had been using benzodiazepines for a range of reasons including anxiety, insomnia and alcohol withdrawal.

Interferon-induced transmembrane protein 3 ( IFITM3) is an antiviral factor that restricts influenza A virus ( IAV) infection in vitro and in vivo.
However, some viruses such as Lassa fever virus ( LASV) have evolved mechanisms to evade this restriction.

We have developed an approach to study the interaction between viruses and host factors using live-cell microscopy, which has revealed that IFITM3 is trafficked from the plasma membrane to intracellular vesicles where it interacts with incoming viruses.
We found that IAV, but not LASV, is trapped in IFITM3-containing endosomes and undergoes hemifusion with the endosomal membrane but fails to deliver its genome into the cytosol.
We found that Lassa fever virus ( LASV) utilizes an alternative endocytosis pathway to enter cells, which is not restricted by IFITM proteins.
Our data suggest that IFITM3 acts as an inhibitor of viral entry by trapping viruses in a hemifused state.

Enterococcus faecalis, a common commensal bacterium found in the gastrointestinal tract and on mucosal surfaces, has evolved a unique mechanism for sensing host-derived signals to express a surface protein called Ace ( Acylated Cell-surface Enzyme) that binds to collagen.

We found that Ace is important for virulence of E. faecalis in an animal model of infection.
Graft-versus-host disease ( GVHD) is a major complication of allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HCT).

Study characteristics We searched scientific databases and conference proceedings to identify studies that randomly assigned people receiving an allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation to either mycophenolate mofetil or methotrexate for the prevention of graft-versus-host disease.
The review authors searched for studies published up to November 2015 and found 10 studies with 11,000 participants.
The studies were of short duration and did not report any serious adverse events.
The study was funded by the National Eye Institute, and the National Eye Research Institute (NEI) of the National Institutes of Health.

Key results There were no significant differences in any of the outcomes examined including incidence of acute graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), relapse, non-relapse mortality, incidence of chronic GVHD, or overall survival.

The FUS/TLS protein is an RNA-binding protein that has been implicated in the pathogenesis of several neurodegenerative diseases.

In this work, we use a novel approach to analyze electroencephalographic recordings from patients with epilepsy and show how it is possible to reconstruct the underlying brain activity using a combination of machine learning techniques and mathematical modeling.
We applied this method to data from twelve patients with focal epilepsy, and found that it was able to identify the seizure onset zone in all cases.
The brain is an extremely complex system and it is difficult to identify the underlying mechanisms responsible for seizure generation.
We found that the brain activity patterns during seizures are highly variable and heterogeneous across patients, but also exhibit common features.
This work demonstrates how mathematical modeling can be used to improve our understanding of the mechanisms underlying epilepsy and to guide clinical practice.
