This work provides new insights into how neuronal circuits are organized in vivo, and may help to explain some aspects of neurological disease.
The ability to mount an effective anti-viral response is dependent on the generation of cytotoxic CD8 T lymphocytes ( CTLs) which are activated in response to viral infection.
We found that IL-21 and IL-15 signals are sufficient to restore the ability of exhausted CD8+ T cells to kill target cells.
In this study, we found that the exhausted phenotype of HBV-specific CD8 T cells in chronic HBV infection is associated with reduced expression of PD-1 and increased expression of TIM-3.
We found that PD-1 expression is increased on HBV-specific CD8+ T cells and that blockade of PD-1 enhances their function.
This study provides insight into how exhausted CD8+ T cells are maintained in chronic infection, and may have implications for vaccine design.
Salmonella Typhimurium ( S. Tm) is an important foodborne pathogen of humans and livestock worldwide.

We found that Salmonella has up to four gtr genes, which are organized in ten different gene clusters ( gtr operons) that encode proteins involved in glycosylation of the bacterial cell surface.
We also found that some genes are expressed in only one or two strains, suggesting that they may be involved in strain-specific virulence mechanisms.
This study demonstrates that the Gtr system is essential for generating the diverse O-antigens found within S. Typhimurium strains.
These findings have implications for the development of vaccines and diagnostics for this disease.

We show that the monitoring of these variables could provide an early warning system for the occurrence of outbreaks, but this would require a trade-off to be made between the number of sites monitored and the frequency of sampling.

Here, we propose a computational model for auxin-driven leaf pattern formation in Arabidopsis thaliana.

Our results show that the model is able to reproduce many features of auxin transport in Arabidopsis thaliana, and we use it to predict how changes in the molecular mechanisms underlying auxin transport affect the overall pattern of auxin distribution in the plant.
Our model is able to reproduce many features of auxin transport in Arabidopsis thaliana, including the polar transport of auxin along the stem and the formation of leaf primordia.

We also found that there were some problems with how we measured frailty, which may affect the results.

Here we show that the JAK1/2 inhibitor tofacitinib is able to reduce the size of the HIV reservoir by blocking the establishment of latency and maintaining latently infected cells in an active state.
We found that JAK inhibition blocks HIV replication and prevents virus spread in vivo by blocking IL-15-induced viral reactivation from latency.
We found that Jak inhibition reduced the size of the HIV reservoir in both CD4+ central memory and effector memory T cells from infected individuals.

We found that mutations in histone H3 or H4 are lethal when combined with mutations in the BAH domain of Sir3p.
We show that this interaction is required for silencing and that it involves a specific region on Sir3p, which we call the LRS ( linker region).

The proper segregation of chromosomes in meiosis requires the formation of at least one crossover per pair of homologous chromosomes, which is mediated by the enzyme DSB1 ( also known as Mre11).

The synaptonemal complex ( SC) is a proteinaceous structure that assembles on chromosomes during early prophase I of meiosis, and it is thought to play an important role in coordinating the processes of homolog pairing and crossover recombination.
In this study we show that the Zip1 protein, which is essential for crossover formation in budding yeast, has an additional function as a regulator of the meiotic recombination machinery.
In this study we show that the SUMOylation of Ecm11 is required for its function in meiotic recombination.
We show that Zip1 is required for the proper localization of Zip3, a protein involved in meiotic recombination, to the synaptonemal complex ( SC) and to recombination hotspots.

We show that Zip1 is required for proper localization of the meiotic recombination machinery to the synaptonemal complex ( SC) and that Zip1 is essential for the formation of crossover sites in the absence of DSBs.
We find that Zip1 has two functionally distinct domains, which may enable it to act as a liaison between the two processes.

We have developed an efficient method to express recombinant Leishmania donovani ThrRS ( rLdThrRS) in Escherichia coli, which is used for studying the function of this enzyme.
We have previously shown that the Leishmania donovani ThrRS is essential for parasite survival and virulence, but its function has not been studied.

Chromatin structure plays an important role in regulating gene expression, but how this occurs remains poorly understood.

We use computer simulations to study how the genome folds and how it is organized within the nucleus, by simulating the folding of a large piece of DNA ( chromosome 19) with thousands of genes.
We find that genes with similar expression patterns tend to be located close together on the chromosome, suggesting that the spatial organization of the genome is related to its function.

We find that CHD8 is enriched at promoters of genes involved in cell cycle regulation and that it is required for efficient transcriptional activation of these genes.
We found that CHD8 is required for the recruitment of RNA polymerase II to enhancers.
Here, we describe a method to generate a library of lipids that can be used to deliver mRNA to cells.
We have developed an amphiphilic charge-altering releasable transporter ( CART) platform for targeted delivery of mRNA therapeutics.
Here we report that certain combinations of these lipids can be used to create highly efficient and safe mRNA delivery systems for gene expression in primary human T cells.
We demonstrate that these lipids enhance gene delivery to T cells, both in vitro and in vivo.
Staphylococcus aureus is a common cause of infections in humans, and it has developed resistance to many antibiotics.
Staphylococcus aureus is an opportunistic pathogen that causes a wide range of infections in humans.
We used transposon sequencing to identify genes required for S. aureus survival in mouse models of infection, and found that many of these were involved in cell wall synthesis or maintenance.
We found that S. aureus infection of mice leads to decreased oxygen levels within the bone microenvironment.
We found that S. aureus lacking SrrA and SrrB is more virulent in mice with compromised immune systems, suggesting that SrrA and SrrB are important for survival within host tissues.
We found that S. aureus uses its SsrB protein to sense changes in oxygen levels and adjust its behavior accordingly.
Our study demonstrates that S. aureus uses multiple mechanisms to sense and respond to oxygen levels in the host environment.
The mitochondrial inner membrane is highly folded to increase its surface area and facilitate the efficient transfer of protons across it.
Here, we report on the structure of an intact mitochondrial F1F0-ATP synthase dimer from the green alga Polytomella sp.
We show that these proteins form rows of dimers, which bend the membrane to create a nanoscale pore.
The F1F0-ATP synthase is an essential enzyme in mitochondria, which catalyzes the synthesis of ATP from ADP and Pi.
Severe fever with renal syndrome ( SFRS) is an emerging infectious disease in China, which was first identified in 2010.

We found that immunization with recombinant NSs protein from either genotype 1 or 2 induced a strong humoral and cellular immune response in mice, which protected them against challenge with heterologous genotypes of SFTS virus.

Malnutrition is common among patients with cancer, especially those who are elderly or have advanced disease.
The review found that giving dietary advice alone or adding nutritional supplements to dietary advice did not improve overall mortality (death rate) compared with no intervention.
Study characteristics We searched for studies comparing dietary advice with or without nutritional supplements to no advice, dietary advice only or nutritional supplements alone.
The results showed that people who received dietary advice had a significantly lower body mass index (BMI) after one year compared with those who did not receive any advice.
There is some evidence to suggest that nutritional interventions may be effective in improving muscle strength and reducing body fat.
More research is needed to determine whether or not nutritional interventions improve the quality of life for people with dementia.

We show that ID4 is a marker of aggressive disease, and we provide evidence that targeting lysosomes may be an effective strategy to treat metastatic disease.
RNase E is an endoribonuclease that degrades mRNA in Escherichia coli, but its function has not been studied in other bacteria.
The bacterial enzyme polynucleotide phosphorylase ( PNPase) is an essential component of the RNA degradation machinery in E. coli.

We found that the p53 protein is acetylated at multiple lysine residues in response to different stimuli, and that these modifications are important for its function as a tumor suppressor.

We found that pyocyanin, a secondary metabolite produced by Pseudomonas aeruginosa, is able to inhibit the formation of fungal biofilms and wrinkled colonies in vitro.

The estimation of the basic reproduction number ( R0) is an important task in the study of infectious diseases, but it has been shown to be difficult when using traditional methods.
We propose an ABC method for estimating the parameters of such models from data consisting of a sample of sequences sampled at different time points.
We compared our method with other approaches in simulations and found it to be more accurate than most others for many parameter combinations.

We also show that our approach is able to provide more accurate estimates of the transmission parameters than standard methods when applied to real-world data from the recent Ebola outbreak in West Africa.
Our results show that ABC is a powerful tool for analyzing large phylogenetic data sets.

The CRISPR-Cas9 system has revolutionized molecular biology by enabling precise gene editing and regulation of gene expression.
We have determined structures of Cas9 in complex with its guide RNA and target DNA, which reveal how Cas9 recognizes and cleaves double-stranded DNA.
This work provides a new tool to control Cas9 activity and may be useful in future gene editing applications.
Taenia solium ( pork tapeworm) is a parasitic flatworm that infects humans and pigs in rural areas of sub-Saharan Africa.
In this study we evaluated the impact of an educational program on the knowledge of people living in an area with high prevalence of Chagas disease.
We found that the educational program increased knowledge and attitudes towards pigs, but did not affect behavior or practices related to pork consumption.
The results showed that knowledge about Taenia solium improved over time, and that this improvement was associated with increased access to health care services.

Epigenetic mechanisms, such as DNA methylation, are important for silencing transposable elements ( TEs) in mammalian genomes.

In this study, we show that the histone methyltransferase SET DOMAIN GROUP 1 ( SDG1), which catalyzes the addition of trimethylation to lysine 9 on histone H3 ( H3K9me3), is required for maintaining high levels of H3K4me2/3 and RNA-directed DNA methylation ( RdDM).

Manual therapy for chronic asthma in adults and children Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) on the use of manual therapy techniques to improve lung function, symptoms and quality of life in people with chronic asthma.
The manual therapy group included physiotherapists, respiratory therapists, chiropractors and osteopaths.
We found no evidence that manual therapy helps improve lung function or symptoms in people with asthma.
Restless legs syndrome ( RLS) is a condition in which people feel an urge to move their legs and arms due to uncomfortable sensations such as tingling or burning.
People with restless legs syndrome ( RLS) often report feeling tired, irritable or depressed and they may also experience sleep problems.
There are various treatments available for RLS, including medications (such as opioids) and lifestyle changes (such as exercise).
We found seven studies with moderate to high risk of bias, and we could not combine their results.

We found that patients who received chloroquine had significantly lower parasite loads at day 7 than those receiving placebo.
We found no significant differences in the time to resolution of NS1 antigenaemia, which was similar between groups.

We found no evidence to support this hypothesis, but we observed a trend toward fewer severe cases of dengue hemorrhagic fever among patients treated with chloroquine.
However, CQ is associated with more side effects than placebo and should be used cautiously in people who are at risk of bleeding.

Key results We found that using multiple injections instead of a single injection reduced the risk of anaesthesia failure by 75%.

The use of different types of needles for reducing post-dural puncture headache after lumbar puncture Background Post-dural puncture headache (PDPH) is a complication of lumbar puncture, which occurs in approximately 1% to 5% of patients undergoing this procedure.

The review authors searched the literature and found 11 studies, which included 10 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and one non-randomised study.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to September 2015.

The review found that using atraumatic needles may reduce the risk of developing post-dural headache after lumbar puncture by about two-thirds compared with standard needles.

Integrated information theory ( IIT) is a theoretical framework for understanding consciousness, which has been successful in explaining many aspects of conscious experience and has been supported by numerous experiments.
We propose a set of axiomatic principles that describe how these mechanisms should be configured in order to produce conscious experience.

The theory posits that consciousness arises from the integrated information generated by the causal interactions within a system, and that this integrated information is encoded as a maximal irreducibility of its internal states to external stimuli.

We show how this approach leads to several testable predictions, including: ( i) consciousness is not necessarily related to complexity or activity in the brain; ( ii) consciousness is not necessarily related to the external environment; ( iii) consciousness is not necessarily related to the number of neurons involved; ( iv) consciousness is not necessarily related to the amount of information processed by the brain; ( v) consciousness is not necessarily related to the speed at which information is processed; Schistosomes are parasitic worms that infect humans and other mammals through the skin.

We find that this is not always the case, and that in some cases the system may evolve towards a stable state where only one of the strategies survives.
In this study, we have used a combination of metabolomics and proteomics to identify the metabolic changes occurring in Mtb during infection.

We found that the transcription factor WhiB3 controls the synthesis of several important lipids in Mycobacterium tuberculosis ( Mtb) including the complex virulence polyketide polyacyltrehalose ( PAT).

Here we report on the development of a method to label mycobacteria with radioactive lipids and demonstrate that this approach allows us to identify novel regulators of lipid metabolism in Mycobacterium tuberculosis ( Mtb).
We show that WhiB3 is required for maintaining intracellular redox balance during infection by scavenging reducing equivalents generated by host cells.
This study provides a novel insight into the role of WhiB3 in Mycobacterium tuberculosis metabolism and its adaptation to host environment.

X-ray free-electron lasers ( XFELs) are powerful tools for studying the structure of materials at the atomic scale, but their high intensity and short pulse duration make it difficult to study molecular dynamics in real time.
Here, we use ultrafast X-ray scattering to study the dynamics of water during its transition from liquid to plasma.
We show that this phenomenon occurs in a water system that is rapidly ionized and driven far from equilibrium, which we simulate using molecular dynamics and plasma dynamics.
We have identified a novel mutation in SAMD9L associated with a rare form of neonatal-onset inflammatory bowel disease ( NODIBD).
We identified two patients with leukocytoclastic panniculitis, basal ganglia calcification, and other clinical features consistent with systemic inflammation who had de novo mutations in SAMD9L.
We found that SAMD9L is an inhibitor of protein synthesis and that it acts by binding to ribosomes.
We found that SAMD9L is expressed in all blood cells and that its expression is reduced in patients with ataxia-pancytopenia syndrome ( APS) caused by a missense mutation in SAMD9L.
Echinococcus granulosus is one of the most important zoonotic parasites worldwide, and its control has been difficult to achieve due to the lack of an effective vaccine.
The current study describes the development and evaluation of a new serological test for canine leishmaniasis diagnosis.
We have developed an assay for detecting S. stercoralis infection using a monoclonal antibody ( MAbEg9) that binds to a unique epitope on the parasite surface.
The test is easy to perform and transportable, as it does not require any specialized equipment or refrigeration during transportation.
In this study we evaluated the role of different species of sand fly in the transmission of Leishmania infantum in an endemic area of Brazil.
We studied the diversity of sand flies in the Phlebotomus genus, which is the vector of Leishmania infantum in Morocco.
We found that the sand fly fauna of the study area is dominated by the genus Sergentomyia and that the rodhaini complex is relatively rare.

We found that Leishmania infantum is present in both dogs and humans, but not in sand flies, and that the presence of L. schwetzi in sand flies is associated with the presence of antibodies to this species in dogs and humans.
The aim of this review was to assess whether or not there are any benefits and harms for women with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) who receive insulin therapy during pregnancy.
The review found that there are many ways to screen for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) but no clear evidence on which method is better.
We looked at how well these tests work, whether they are acceptable to women and whether or not they would be good value for money.
The freshwater animal Hydra has been used as an experimental model for studying cancer since the early 20th century.
This study provides evidence that the intestinal microbiota is important for maintaining normal epithelial cell function, and that its absence may lead to increased susceptibility to infection.
Our results suggest that the mechanisms underlying this process are conserved from worms to humans and may be relevant to human cancers.
We use a coarse-grained protein force field to investigate the stability of amyloid-b protein ( 1-40) as it aggregates from monomers to octamers.
The formation of amyloid fibrils is a common feature of many neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer's disease ( AD).
We find that the free energy landscape of the protein is nearly flat and has no barrier to aggregation.
We find that the mutation increases the stability of the monomer, but decreases the stability of the fibril.
The formation of synapses is a complex process involving multiple molecular players.
However, the molecular mechanisms underlying this process remain largely unknown.
However, little is known about how T cells respond to stimuli with varying durations and intensities.
Here, we show that optogenetic stimulation of CAR T cells using pulsed light can be used to control their activity in vivo and in vitro.

Our results suggest that the immune system has evolved to be able to sense and respond to time-varying stimuli, which may have important implications for understanding how the immune system interprets complex environmental cues.
We find that there are only a few possible measures that satisfy all the requirements and that they are very similar to each other.
Transverse myelitis ( TM) is a rare inflammatory disease of the spinal cord which may be caused by infection or other factors such as autoimmune disorders.
Neuromyelitis Optica ( NMO), also known as Devic's disease, is a rare autoimmune disorder of the central nervous system ( CNS).
However, this treatment has some disadvantages including being expensive and difficult to administer.
We compared two groups of children who received either steroids alone or steroids and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG).
The study was designed to see if there is evidence of benefit from surgery for people who have had an incomplete spinal cord injury and are unable to walk independently.
We recruited two people to take part in the trial but they did not complete it.
This study highlights the need for further research to improve the quality and quantity of data available on the use of antibiotics in the treatment of leprosy.
Osteoradionecrosis (ORN) is a severe side effect of radiotherapy for head and neck cancers.
The condition is associated with significant morbidity, including pain, disability, and loss of independence.
ORN is a serious and costly disease in cattle, but there is no vaccine available for prevention or treatment.

We wanted to find out whether there are ways to prevent this condition from occurring, by looking at studies that have investigated treatments given before surgery or radiotherapy to reduce the risk of developing ORN.
Key results We found four relevant studies with a total of 131 participants who had undergone radiotherapy for head and neck cancer.
One study compared fluoride gel with high-fluoride toothpaste for preventing radiation-induced oral disease.
The quality of the evidence was low to very low for all outcomes due to poor study designs and small numbers of participants.
However, more research is needed to determine which type of exercise is most effective for improving symptoms and quality of life in people with COPD.
We present two new biologically inspired neuron models that incorporate metabolic processes to explain experimental data from different types of neurons.

We show that our model is able to capture a wide range of experimental observations and predict several important features of neuronal dynamics that have been observed in experiments but not previously explained by other models.
Shaving pubic hair before childbirth (birth) may reduce the risk of infection after a cut to the perineum (perineal wound).
The first study compared two different methods of preparing the skin around the vagina (perineum) before surgery; one group had their vulval hair clipped and the other group was shaved.
The condition is not only physically disabling but also causes psychological distress due to social exclusion and stigma.
This is particularly true for patients with chronic diseases, such as cancer or HIV/AIDS, who have to cope with multiple stressors in their lives.
The disease is endemic in many tropical countries, but little research has been done to explore the psychological impact of the disease on those who are infected.
We found that, in this population, depression was common but not more common than other mental health problems.
Depression is common among people who have lymphatic filariasis, but it has not been studied well enough to know how best to treat it.
Genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) have been successful in identifying common variants associated with many diseases.
However, it remains unclear whether this is true and if so, what proportion of genetic variation in disease risk is explained by rare variants.
However, it has been difficult to develop models that capture the complexity of this process because we lack knowledge of how phenotypes evolve over time.

We use simulations to show how this approach can be used to identify genes that contribute to disease risk by identifying regions of the genome where there is an excess of rare variants that are likely to be harmful.
We show that this is not true and that a simple test based on the distribution of rare variants in affected individuals can be used to identify disease-causing mutations with high power.
The Nrf2 pathway has been implicated in the pathogenesis of AD.
This study provides insight into the molecular mechanism by which Nrf2 is inhibited and suggests that this pathway could be exploited to prevent neurodegeneration.
We show that pharmacological inhibition of Keap1 prevents Ab42-induced neurotoxicity and promotes survival of neurons expressing mutant tau protein.
We show that this mechanism is conserved in human neurons and that it can be targeted to prevent synaptopathy.
Our results suggest that targeting Keap1 may be a promising therapeutic strategy to prevent or slow down the progression of AD.

Phylogenetic comparative methods have been widely used to study the evolution of morphological traits in animals, but they often assume that trait values evolve according to Gaussian distributions ( e. g., Brownian motion).

We present a new method, called Mixed Gaussian Phylogenetic Models ( MGPM), which combines the advantages of both approaches and provides an alternative to existing methods for reconstructing ancestral states from sequence data.
The ability to recognize familiar individuals is essential for social interaction, but how this ability changes with experience remains unclear.
We found that, unlike neurons in other parts of the brain, these cells showed little to no changes in their response properties over a period of one year.
IkBa is a key regulator of the NF-kB pathway, which plays a central role in many biological processes such as immune response, inflammation, cell proliferation and cancer.
We show that this model reproduces a wide range of experimental data, including thermodynamic and kinetic properties of the protein folding/unfolding process.
We show how this sequence disorder affects the stability and dynamics of the protein by initiating a cascade of structural changes that propagate through the protein.
This study provides an example of how disordered regions can be used as a tool for protein engineering.

Our results suggest that UN aid is driven by humanitarian concerns and not by strategic interests.

We find that multilateral aid allocations are less responsive to recipient country characteristics than bilateral aid, suggesting that multilateral aid may be more insulated from political considerations.

The intestinal epithelium is one of the most rapidly renewing tissues in mammals, with cells being replaced every few days.
Cancer stem cells ( CSCs) have been implicated in tumor initiation and relapse, but their origin remains unclear.
We find that, under certain conditions, even small increases in the probability of asymmetric division can lead to rapid expansion of mutant stem cells.
The Mycobacterium tuberculosis ( Mtb) cell envelope is composed of a complex lipid-rich outer membrane and an inner peptidoglycan layer.
The bacterial cell wall is composed of peptidoglycan ( PG) and lipopolysaccharide ( LPS), which are synthesized by different enzymes located in the inner and outer membranes, respectively.

The rapid growth in genomic data has led to the development of many methods for identifying genes associated with disease, but these methods have been limited by their reliance on existing knowledge about gene function and expression patterns.
The identification of genes within genomic sequences is an important step in understanding how cells function and how they respond to environmental changes.
G-OnRamp is a web-based tool for creating custom genome browsers from any organism's genomic sequence data.
We have developed an open-source web application called G-OnRamp that allows users to upload genomic data from any organism and use it for educational purposes or as part of a research project.
We describe how we developed genome browsers for four parasitoid wasps, which were then used to engage undergraduate students with genomics research.
The enzymes involved in peptidoglycan synthesis are called penicillin-binding proteins ( PBPs).
The pneumococcus is an important human pathogen that causes invasive disease in children and adults.
We have previously shown that the class B PBPs are involved in the regulation of cell wall synthesis, but the role of class A PBPs has remained unclear.
We found that the class A penicillin-binding protein ( PBP) CbpD is essential for pneumococcal growth and survival in vivo, but not in vitro.
Ozone in the lower atmosphere ( troposphere) is a major air pollutant and greenhouse gas.
We present a record of iodine concentrations from an Alpine ice core, which shows that iodine levels have increased by ~50% over the last century.
We use a global chemistry-transport model to demonstrate that this is caused by changes in atmospheric chemistry driven by increasing nitrogen oxides emissions from biomass burning.
We find that the model underestimates the observed iodine deposition trend and that this is likely due to an underestimate of the 20th-century ozone increase.

The protein product of the Huntingtin gene, which contains an expanded glutamine repeat in Huntington's disease patients, has been shown to be toxic and cause cell death when expressed in cultured cells.
Here, we show that expanded huntingtin forms both amorphous and fibrillar aggregates in cells expressing the N-terminal fragment of mutant huntingtin ( HttEx1).
Our results suggest that polyglutamine toxicity is not only due to protein misfolding but also to the formation of toxic aggregates that disrupt cellular homeostasis.
Neurocysticercosis ( NCC) is a parasitic infection caused by Taenia solium eggs that are ingested through contaminated food or water.
We found that women are more likely than men to be affected by NCC, but that they have less access to treatment and care for this disease.
Sexual reproduction is an essential biological process in most eukaryotes, including humans.
Spermatogonial stem cells ( SSCs) are responsible for generating sperm in mammals.
We show that NF-YB is required for stem cell maintenance in planarians, but not for their specification.
We also show that the NF-Y complex is conserved in the parasitic flatworm Schistosoma mansoni and that it has similar functions as in C. elegans.
Our results show that NF-YB plays an essential role in the development and maintenance of the male reproductive system, which is critical for transmission of schistosome parasites to their hosts.
These results provide new insights into the biology of adult worms and may be useful in developing novel control strategies against schistosome infections.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a common cause of hospital-acquired infections in immunocompromised patients.
However, it remains unclear whether RsmA regulates gene expression at the level of transcription or post-transcriptionally.
In bacteria, transcription and translation are coupled such that proteins can bind to nascent transcripts before they have completed synthesis.
Mutations in the RAS oncogene are among the most common genetic alterations in cancer.
These results provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying RAS-driven cancer progression, which may lead to novel therapeutic strategies.

We show that this approach is effective against multiple strains of HIV-1 and allows for the generation of long-lasting protective immunity to infection with simian/human immunodeficiency virus ( SHIV) in rhesus macaques.
Our results suggest that passive immunization with broadly neutralizing antibodies may be an effective strategy to induce long-lasting protection against HIV-1 infection.

We found that antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity ( ADCC) is essential for protection against infection with this virus, and that it plays a key role in the generation of long-term immune memory to the virus.
These results suggest that antibody-mediated killing of infected cells is important to elicit effective CD8+ T cell responses against HIV-1 infection.
Vector control is the main strategy to prevent dengue transmission, but it has been difficult to achieve in many countries due to lack of resources and poor infrastructure.
Wolbachia bacteria are being used in an innovative strategy to control dengue virus ( DENV) transmission by mosquitoes.

The release of Wolbachia-infected mosquitoes in an urban area of Brazil resulted in a significant reduction in dengue transmission and no adverse events were reported to the health authorities during the study period.

We show that Wolbachia is not able to invade a field population with high levels of pyrethroid resistance, and that the use of Wolbachia in combination with pyrethroids will be needed to control dengue transmission in this setting.
However, previous studies have found little support for this hypothesis, possibly due to methodological limitations.
We studied how wild capuchin monkeys ( Cebus capucinus) interacted with each other and their environment over 14 months in a tropical forest.
We show that fruit-eating birds in the tropics have higher intake rates of fruits with conspicuous colors ( i.e., red, yellow, orange) compared to those with inconspicuous colors ( i.e., green).
Our results provide strong support for the idea that trichromacy is an adaptation to fruit eating, and they suggest that this may be especially important when fruits are eaten at close range.
To extract self-motion information from the visual input, the brain must solve this ill-posed problem.
We have developed a computational model of how the fly's nervous system processes information from its compound eyes to guide flight in response to moving objects.
We demonstrate that this model is able to detect motion in a wide range of natural scenes with different textures, lighting conditions and object velocities.
This work is relevant to the design of autonomous systems for both military and civilian use.

Here we describe exceptionally well-preserved fossils from the ~430-Ma Herefordshire Lagerstatte ( UK) that reveal the presence of an undescribed species of millipede with a unique combination of features.
We describe a new fossil arthropod, which is preserved in amber from Myanmar and has ten small arthropods attached to it.
The diagnosis of AE is challenging because it relies on clinical symptoms and imaging studies with limited sensitivity.
In this study we describe a new method to detect E. multilocularis DNA in human liver biopsies using real-time quantitative PCR ( qPCR).

The results showed that the sensitivity of the immunohistochemistry test is higher than the molecular biology technique when the sample contains spemocytes but not when it contains only schistosome eggs.
We found no evidence for the presence of viable parasites in these structures, and we conclude that they are likely to be dead parasites that have been engulfed by host cells.
The molecular mechanisms underlying daily rhythms in gene expression have been studied extensively; however, how these rhythms are regulated at the level of chromatin is not well understood.
The molecular mechanisms underlying circadian rhythms are complex and involve multiple proteins that regulate gene expression through posttranslational modification of histones.
We show here that the Brm chromatin remodeling complex is required for maintaining circadian rhythms in Drosophila melanogaster.

We find that BRM is required for maintaining high levels of histone H3K9 methylation in the morning, which creates a repressive chromatin environment that limits transcriptional output from the clock genes.
We found that BRM has both catalytic and non-catalytic roles in regulating clock gene expression.
We have used a genetically engineered mouse model to show that loss of ATF2 expression in melanoma cells leads to reduced tumor growth and metastasis.
We found that the majority of these mice develop melanoma, but when we expressed mutant ATF2 specifically in melanocytes, only one out of twenty-one mice developed melanoma.
We found that MITF is regulated by ATF2 in mouse skin and human melanoma cells.
We found that ATF2 is required for oncogenic BRAF(V600E)-induced transformation in vitro and in vivo.
These results provide new insights into the role of ATF2 in melanoma progression and suggest that targeting ATF2 may be an effective strategy for treating melanoma.
The ability of multiple species to coexist in a given environment is known as biodiversity, and it has been studied extensively in ecology for decades.
The question of how species coexist is central to ecology and evolutionary biology.
We present a new method for detecting and quantifying such patterns, which is robust to noise and does not rely on arbitrary thresholds or binning of data.
Our results suggest that cluster formation is a ubiquitous feature of biological systems and may be used to improve understanding of gene regulation in other organisms.
Cannabinoids are known to have anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective properties, which make them potential candidates for treating neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease ( AD).
We show that the antinociceptive effect of THC is mediated by 5-HT2A receptors, whereas its amnesic effect is mediated by cannabinoid CB1 receptors.
We found that these receptors interact with each other to form functional receptor complexes that are present in specific brain areas associated with cognitive decline.

These results suggest that CB1R-5-HT2A receptor heteromers may be an attractive target for developing new therapeutics with reduced psychoactive side effects and improved efficacy in treating pain, depression, and anxiety disorders.
We show that languages with larger speaker populations are less likely to undergo rapid change.
The study of language evolution is hampered by the fact that languages have been spoken for thousands of years, making it difficult to determine how long ago they diverged from one another.
Insecticide treated targets are used to control tsetse fly populations in Africa, but their effectiveness is reduced when vegetation grows up around them.
This study shows that the current standard method of using a single dose of radiation to treat cancer may not be sufficient, and that multiple doses are needed to ensure that all tumors are killed.
The results showed that the combination of these two technologies is effective in controlling tsetse fly populations and has potential to be used as an alternative to insecticide spraying.
We show that the presence of gaps in vegetation, which are common in natural landscapes, can lead to the emergence of complex spatiotemporal patterns of disease spread.

We conclude that the current standard practice of using a 1 m radius clearance zone around treated targets may not be necessary for Palpalis group species and that a smaller clearance zone could be used to reduce costs associated with control efforts.
Aging is associated with progressive loss of muscle mass and strength, which contributes to frailty and disability.
We have used a forward genetics approach to identify genes that affect behavior in aging flies.
We used the nematode C. elegans to study how aging affects behavior and identified a gene called unc-13 as being important for maintaining normal locomotion with age.
We found that loss of raga-1, which encodes a Ras-like small GTPase, extends lifespan and improves locomotor function in aged animals.
We found that RAGA-1 is required for longevity in C. elegans and that its loss of function increases lifespan.
We show that these mutations have profound effects on behavior, including increased aggression and reduced social interaction, and also affect lifespan.
Genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) have been widely used for identifying genetic variants associated with complex diseases.
We propose an approach called Multiple Loci Linear Mixed Model ( MLMM) for identifying causal variants by incorporating multiple markers simultaneously in the model.
We have developed an efficient algorithm to analyze large-scale GWAS data in parallel on a cluster of computers.
We show that, in addition to punishing free-riders directly, individuals also punish those who are not punished by others.
However, when the user's intent is not directly observable, it becomes difficult to train the model to decode their intentions.
We propose a new method to train such decoders using only the output of the oracle and the input data.
We show that a simple algorithm for machine learning from demonstrations can be used to learn a generic BCI system that is robust and generalizable across subjects.
However, the impact of climate change on forest ecosystems is not well understood.
The Mediterranean region is expected to experience increased climate extremes in the coming decades.
Our results highlight the importance of conserving relict tree populations in mountainous regions, which are likely to be threatened by climate change.
TLX is a transcription factor expressed in the brain, including the hippocampus, which is involved in learning and memory.
We have developed a transgenic mouse model to investigate the role of TLX in the brain.
We found that TLX promotes neurogenesis by increasing the number of newborn neurons, which is mediated through its direct regulation of the Wnt/b-catenin signaling pathway.
We found that the loss of TLX in the developing brain causes severe defects in the generation and maintenance of neurons.
The TLX transgenic mice showed improved cognitive function compared to wild-type mice.
In this study we show that the absence of MKP-2 in macrophages results in an inability to control L. major infection and that this is due to a failure to induce IL-10 production by infected cells.
In this study we show that mice deficient in MKP-2 are not impaired in their ability to control Leishmania major infections.
We found that MKP-2 is required for optimal control of Leishmania major infection by IFN-g activated macrophages.
Iron-sulfur ( Fe-S) clusters are essential cofactors found in many enzymes involved in diverse biological processes.
In this study, we show that GRXS15 is required for the assembly of aconitase, an enzyme involved in the citric acid cycle, which is essential for energy production in mitochondria.
We found that GRXS15 is required for iron-sulfur cluster assembly in mitochondria and that it coordinates with other proteins involved in mitochondrial iron-sulfur cluster biogenesis.
Here, we show that an Arabidopsis thaliana gene, GRXS15, is required for iron-sulfur cluster biogenesis in plants and that it is essential for plant development.
We identified key amino acids in GRXS15 required for its function and demonstrated that these are conserved across evolution.
We found that GRXS15 is required for the function of iron-sulfur proteins and that it is involved in the regulation of iron homeostasis.
Atrial fibrillation ( AF) is an irregular heart rhythm, which increases the risk of stroke and blood clots.
Anticoagulants are drugs used to prevent blood clots from forming in veins.
Newer anticoagulants are safer and easier to use than warfarin but may be less effective in preventing stroke.
Anticoagulants are drugs that prevent blood clotting and so reduce the risk of thrombosis (blood clots).
The study found that, compared with usual care, adding exercise to the treatment of people with heart failure improved quality of life but did not reduce death or hospital admissions.
Salmonella uses two type III secretion systems ( T3SS) to infect and survive within mammalian hosts.
The Salmonella pathogenicity island ( SPI) encodes a type III secretion system ( T3SS) that injects bacterial virulence factors directly into host cells to promote infection.
Inflammasome activation has been implicated in host defense against bacterial infections, but its role in Salmonella infection remains unknown.
We show that this is due to the ability of these two inflammasomes to sense bacterial infection and induce caspase-1-dependent IL-1b secretion.
We found that Salmonella triggers activation of two different inflammasome complexes, the NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome and the NLRP3 inflammasome, to induce caspase-1-dependent IL-1b secretion.
Arthropod-borne viruses ( arboviruses) are transmitted by mosquitoes and ticks, but their evolutionary origins remain unclear.
The family Bunyaviridae comprises many important human pathogens such as Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus ( CCHFV) and Rift Valley Fever virus ( RVFV).
We found that both viruses are genetically distinct and distant from all other known viruses.
In this paper we propose a method to analyze time-course gene expression data using gene sets.
We show in simulations that TcGSA has better performance than existing methods for detecting differential expression of gene sets over time.
We provide a software implementation of this approach in the form of an R package, which allows users to apply it to their own data.
We show that the mevalonate pathway is required for tumor growth by promoting macropinocytosis of extracellular matrix proteins.
We found that GGPP is required for pseudopod formation, macropinocytosis, and endocytosis.
Wolbachia bacteria are endosymbionts of many insect species and are used for biological control of mosquito vectors of human diseases such as dengue fever and Zika virus.
We have sequenced over 70 kb of the genome of wOvo, a Wolbachia strain infecting the human parasite Onchocerca volvulus.
We show that the Wolbachia genome has undergone significant rearrangements in different species, but that some regions have been conserved over millions of years.
We found evidence for horizontal gene transfer from Wolbachia to Onchocerca and also discovered that the Wolbachia genome has been integrated into the Onchocerca genome.
We show that this adaptation occurred before the split between the human and cattle parasites, suggesting that it was an important factor in the transition to humans.
Understanding how cells decide which fate they will adopt is essential for understanding normal development, as well as many diseases such as cancer.
We present two new methods to identify regulatory elements in genomic data by analyzing the expected pattern of expression from a set of genes.
We found no evidence for spatial clustering of cases, suggesting that most infections are acquired from outside the area where they were diagnosed.
We also identified a significant association between the genetic lineage of the bacteria and the severity of disease in patients.
This study shows that C. neoformans is a phenotypically diverse species with different lineages having distinct virulence properties.
These findings provide insight into how oncogenic KRAS promotes tumor growth and metastasis in lung adenocarcinoma and identify potential therapeutic targets to treat this disease.
The ability to perceive the location and distance of touch is essential for our sense of body ownership, spatial awareness, and even social interaction.
We show that the brain represents tactile space in a way that is consistent with a simple geometric model of touch perception.
We demonstrate that the Drosophila ortholog of human NUDT15 has two distinct functions in DNA repair and RNA splicing.
We also found that SPF45 interacts with Rad201, a homolog of the bacterial RecA protein, which is involved in recombination repair.
Our results suggest that SPF45 may have evolved to play a role in DNA repair in all organisms.
Circulating cell-free DNA ( cfDNA) is released from dying and living cells, including cancer cells, into the bloodstream.
The human T-cell leukemia virus type 1 ( HTLV-1) causes two distinct diseases in infected individuals.
We show that Tax binds to and inhibits the activity of the A20 protein, which is an inhibitor of NF-kB activation.
The molecular mechanism by which Tax activates IKK is unknown.
We found that Tax is ubiquitinated by Ubc13 through interaction with cell adhesion molecule 1 ( CADM1), which is recruited to the membrane by Tax.
We found that the C-terminal domain of CADM1, which contains a PDZ-binding motif, is required for Tax-induced NF-kB activation and that this interaction is mediated by the adaptor protein Nck.
We found that Tax interacts with CADM1, which is recruited to the plasma membrane by Tax, where it forms a complex with Ubc13, NEMO, and TAX1BP1 to activate IKK.
Roads are increasingly penetrating the Amazon, and their expansion has been linked to biodiversity loss.
Oncolytic virus therapy has emerged as an innovative approach to cancer treatment, and several oncolytic viruses have been developed for clinical use.
We identified an alphavirus ( M1) that selectively kills ZAP-deficient cancer cells in vitro and in vivo.
Q fever is caused by Coxiella burnetii, which is transmitted to humans through inhalation of aerosolized bacteria from infected animals or contaminated soil.
The bacteria are highly infectious and cause severe disease in humans.
The bacterium Coxiella burnetii causes Q fever, a zoonotic disease in humans and livestock.

In this study, we have used a combination of genetic and biochemical approaches to identify the molecular mechanism by which Q fever bacteria convert from the virulent phase I to the avirulent phase II form.
We found that these mutants have defects in the synthesis of lipopolysaccharide ( LPS) and that they are defective in biofilm formation, motility, and virulence.
We have used a novel genetic approach to identify several genes required for synthesis of this lipid A structure, which is essential for virulence in Q fever.
We show that disruption of two of these genes results in the production of phase II LPS and demonstrate that this phenotype is associated with reduced virulence in mice.
Genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) have been successful in identifying genetic variants associated with disease risk, but they have not yet identified all causal variants.
However, it is unclear whether these results are due to the lack of power or the absence of genetic interactions.
We present a method to test these hypotheses in an efficient way by first testing simpler hypotheses and then moving on to more complex ones.
Our method is shown to be more powerful than existing methods for detecting differential expression in RNA-sequencing data.
This approach provides a powerful tool to study the molecular mechanisms underlying complex diseases such as cancer and neurodegenerative disorders.

The Ediacaran Period ( ~635 to 541 million years ago) is characterized by the appearance of simple multicellular life forms, including the enigmatic Ediacarans, whose fossil record has been interpreted as representing the origin of animal body plans.
We show how this method can be used to compare fossil and extant species in terms of their functional anatomy, providing an alternative to traditional phylogenetic approaches.

Fluorescent probes are widely used to visualize biological processes in living cells, but they often require chemical modification or conjugation to proteins or other molecules.
The Ni-NTA-AC probe is highly specific and sensitive for detecting His-tagged proteins in living cells.

We have developed an approach to visualize large numbers of tagged proteins in living cells using a single fluorescent protein tag, which is based on the principle of fluorescence resonance energy transfer ( FRET).
The dengue virus ( DENV) is an important mosquito-borne pathogen causing severe disease in humans.
The results showed that the incidence of dengue fever in China was increasing from 2000 to 2015, with an average annual increase rate of 6.
We found that viruses from Myanmar, Indonesia, Thailand, Malaysia and Singapore were introduced to China.
We found evidence for multiple introductions of dengue virus serotypes into China and identified several regions where dengue has been transmitted for many years.
The bacterial cell cycle consists of two main phases, namely growth and division.
We have identified an uncharacterized protein, TrcR, which is required to maintain proper timing of gene expression during the bacterial cell cycle.
We show that TrcR is an important regulator of gene expression during the cell cycle, and that it protects cells from two antibiotics that target different steps of RNA synthesis.
The pigment cell-specific protein PMEL forms amyloid fibrils that are essential for melanin deposition and pigmentation.
We have previously shown that mutations in the melanocyte-specific protein PMEL lead to a range of phenotypes from mild to severe depigmentation.
This study provides insight into how genetic variation in the human genome may lead to disease, and it suggests that some of these variants could be used as biomarkers for disease risk.
The ability of CD8+ T cells to recognize and eliminate infected cells is central to controlling viral infections, but how they are activated in vivo remains poorly understood.

We have developed a new approach for quantifying the amount of viral protein presented by MHC molecules on the surface of infected cells, which allows us to measure how much of each viral protein is produced during infection.

Protein hydrolysis of formula for preterm infants Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether using protein hydrolysed formula compared with standard cow's milk-based formula improves outcomes in preterm infants.
Study characteristics We found six randomised controlled studies that compared protein hydrolysate with standard cow's milk formula in preterm babies.
The studies were all small, with a total of 665 participants.
The majority of babies in these studies were born before 34 weeks' gestation (the period from the beginning to the end of pregnancy) and weighed less than 1750 g at birth.

The illegal wildlife trade is one of the most serious threats to biodiversity worldwide and is driving many species towards extinction.

We found that elephant poaching was unsustainable at the population level during this period, with an estimated 40, 000 elephants killed annually.

We compared ten commercial tests with our in-house assays on a panel of 160 samples.

We found that the performance of these tests was highly dependent on the timing of the test relative to the onset of symptoms and that some tests were better suited for early diagnosis ( within 10 days) while others performed better later ( after 20 days).

We show that the interference between the sound waves emitted by a swarm of insects results in echoes with reduced intensity and altered spectral content compared to those produced by individual insects.
We find that bats use a strategy to avoid this problem, which is to increase the bandwidth of their echolocation calls.

The use of long-acting beta2-agonists (LABAs) in children with asthma Asthma is a common condition affecting many people, especially children.

The aim of this review was to find out whether or not self-management, with support from a healthcare professional, helps people with COPD to improve their quality of life and reduce hospital admissions.
The use of technology, such as telemedicine or mobile apps, may help people with asthma to monitor their condition more effectively.
The use of electronic health records (EHRs) has been shown to improve care and reduce costs in many areas of medicine.
We wanted to find out if using telemedicine would be better than standard care for people who have COPD.

We found no clear evidence that home monitoring and feedback from a doctor reduced the risk of an asthma attack requiring a course of oral steroids, a visit to the emergency department or a hospital stay.
Quality of evidence The overall quality of evidence was variable, ranging from moderate to very low.

The use of mindfulness-based interventions for children and adolescents with mental health problems Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether or not mindfulness-based interventions are effective in treating children and adolescents with mental health problems.

The aim of this review was to find out whether or not these types of programmes are effective at preventing mental health problems such as anxiety, depression and conduct disorders (such as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder) in children and adolescents.

We found 142 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared different types of antidepressants against each other or against placebo.

Gefitinib for the treatment of non-small cell lung cancer Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials on the use of gefitinib in people with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).

However, when used as an add-on treatment to chemotherapy it was found to be more effective than chemotherapy alone.

Key results The review found that using targeted drugs to treat EGFR-mutated NSCLC improves overall survival and progression-free survival in patients who have not progressed after initial chemotherapy treatment.
However, there is some evidence from two studies that using gefitinib after chemotherapy may improve overall and progression free survival.

We found no evidence of benefit from using anaesthetic depth monitoring or standard clinical and electronic monitoring during surgery.

We have developed a new tool, called enhanceratlas, which allows users to explore and visualize these data in a user-friendly way.

We found that, compared to usual care, the addition of permethrin cream to the standard treatment for impetigo reduced the number of children with scabies by 50% at 6 months follow-up.
The study showed that mass treatment of entire communities using oral ivermectin was more effective than referral of patients identified during screening to clinics for topical treatment.

Ascites is an abnormal collection of fluid in the abdomen (tummy) and is often associated with liver disease, heart failure or cancer.

Study characteristics We found only one study, which included 245 people with recurrent malignant ascites due to peritoneal dissemination of epithelial ovarian cancer.

The bacteriome is an organ of insect-microbe interaction and has been found in several species of insects that harbor intracellular bacteria.

Tinnitus is a common condition where people hear sounds when there are no external stimuli to cause them.
The aim of this review was to assess whether any treatments are effective in treating tinnitus.
The use of hearing aids to treat tinnitus Review question We reviewed the evidence for the effectiveness of using hearing aids in treating tinnitus (ringing in the ears).
Hearing aids for tinnitus Review question We reviewed the evidence on how well hearing aids work to reduce tinnitus symptoms in people who have both tinnitus and hearing loss.
Study characteristics We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and non-RCTs comparing hearing aids to other treatments in adults with tinnitus and some degree of hearing loss.

Hearing aids may be used in some cases but it is not clear if they help with tinnitus.

The use of statins for preventing cardiovascular disease in people with diabetes Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether or not statin drugs are effective at reducing the risk of death, heart attack, stroke, and other serious health problems in people with diabetes who do not have heart disease but are at increased risk of developing it.

This review found no evidence of benefit for any drug treatment over placebo (dummy treatment) or standard care alone, but there was some evidence that aspirin may be beneficial in preventing heart attacks and strokes in people who have had an initial heart attack.

We found that interventions which included regular planned appointments, patient education and monitoring of risk factors were more likely to be effective in improving cholesterol levels than those that did not include these elements.

The review found that there is some evidence from one study that occupational therapy may help people with dementia maintain their ability to carry out daily activities, but more research is needed.
We wanted to find out if occupational therapy ( OT) could help people who live in care homes after having a stroke to stay active, reduce their risk of falling and improve their quality of life.
We compared the effects of the intervention with usual care ( i. e. no exercise) on quality of life, physical activity and falls in older people living in nursing homes.
We also measured quality of life, depression and mobility in both groups at 6 and 12 months after treatment started.
We found that, compared to usual care, occupational therapy did not help people living in care homes maintain their independence and activities after having a stroke.
We recommend that future research should focus on how best to encourage older people living in care homes to be more active, and how to make it easier for them to do so.
Bicycle helmet use is known to be effective in reducing the number and severity of head injuries for riders involved in crashes with motor vehicles.

The effectiveness of helmet legislation for reducing head injury is uncertain The introduction of helmet legislation has been shown to reduce the number of cyclist deaths and serious injuries in some countries but not others.
The main findings of this review are that there is no evidence to support the effectiveness of laws requiring all cyclists to wear helmets in reducing death and serious injury rates.
We did not find any evidence on whether or not these laws changed how often people cycled, or if they had any other effects.
The results of this review show that laws requiring bicycle helmets are effective at increasing helmet use and reducing head injuries, but they do not appear to affect other types of injury.
There were no studies that measured changes in cycling levels or any other impacts of bike-share schemes.

In this work we study how such a network could be constructed using spike-timing dependent plasticity ( STDP) as learning rule.
Here, we show how a simple learning rule based on temporal reversal of spike-timing dependent plasticity ( rSTDP) can be used to learn sparse representations in a network of spiking neurons.
We find that this is possible if the synaptic plasticity rule has a depression bias, i.e., it favors weakening of synapses over strengthening.
We show that our model reproduces several experimental observations and predicts new ones.

The Cfl1 protein is a transcriptional regulator of virulence in this pathogen.
We have identified Cfl1, an adhesin from Cryptococcus neoformans, as the first fungal adhesin to be characterized at the molecular level.
Our results suggest that morphological plasticity is an important virulence factor in this fungal pathogen.

Our results suggest that MSCI is an important regulatory mechanism for controlling gene expression in postmeiotic cells of the male germ line.

The use of suctioning for preventing complications in people with an artificial airway Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether or not suctioning is effective in reducing complications in people who have had an artificial airway inserted.
Study characteristics We searched for studies comparing different strategies of ETT suctioning in newborn babies receiving mechanical ventilation support.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant randomised controlled trials (RCTs) published up until May 2015, and found 11 studies with a total of 10,000 participants.
Study characteristics We searched for studies in which babies were randomly assigned to have their airway cleaned at different frequencies while on assisted ventilation.
Study characteristics We found only one small trial (97 participants) of preterm babies with very low birth weight (less than 1500 g).
The review found no clear evidence of benefit or harm from using a six-hourly schedule compared with a 12-hourly schedule.

We show that these factors have distinct functions during development of the hindbrain.
We find that the absence of the transcription factor Pdm1 leads to an increase in the number of Ap neurons in the abdominal ganglion and a decrease in the number of Ap neurons in the thorax.
We show that in the Drosophila brain, the expression of the transcription factor Ap is restricted to specific cell types, which we call "Ap cluster cells."
Our work provides new insights into how the genetic information encoded by the genome is translated into specific cell types during development of the nervous system.

Mirror therapy for improving movement in people with upper limb hemiparesis after stroke Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) on the use of mirror therapy to improve arm and hand function in individuals who have had a stroke.

Mirror therapy for improving recovery after stroke Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) to see whether mirror therapy improves recovery of movement and function after stroke.
Mirror therapy is a type of rehabilitation technique in which patients practice movements in front of a mirror to improve their ability to move their affected limb.
The aim of this review was to find out if mirror therapy is effective in improving recovery after stroke.
The review authors found no studies that compared the effects of different types of exercise on pain and function in people with osteoarthritis (OA) of the knee.
Study characteristics We found 62 studies, involving 1982 people, that looked at whether mirror therapy helped to improve recovery after stroke.
We found that mirror therapy improved activities of daily living and pain in people with stroke, but not their walking ability or motor performance.
We found some evidence that mirror therapy may be effective in improving arm movement, functional ability, and pain in people with hemiparesis (weakness on one side of the body) after stroke.
The studies were limited by small numbers of participants and poor study design.

We found 14 studies that looked at combinations of medication and behavioural support in people who wanted to quit smoking.
Study characteristics We searched for all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of people with schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder who were treated with antipsychotics in an outpatient setting.
The study also provided participants with nicotine gum to use during the programme and for six months after it ended.
We found high quality evidence that combining medication and behaviour therapy is more effective than usual care or brief advice in reducing alcohol consumption.
We found that people who were recruited from healthcare settings had more severe depression and anxiety than those who were recruited from the general public.

Combining medication with behavioural support increases the chances of quitting, but it is unclear whether combining two types of medication (bupropion and nicotine) will help more people quit than one type of medication alone.

PfEMP1 proteins are expressed on the surface of P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes and mediate adhesion to endothelial cells in the microvasculature, which is thought to be an important step in the pathogenesis of severe malaria.
We have previously shown that the PfEMP1 protein family is highly diverse and that this diversity is maintained by recombination between different PfEMP1 genes within individual parasites.
We found that almost all of these parasites are able to bind to the host cell surface protein CD36, which is known to be involved in the invasion process.
We have identified a single amino acid residue in the cysteine-rich interdomain region ( CIDR) domain of CD36 that is critical for binding to fatty acids and lipids.
We have used this information to predict the three-dimensional structure of the entire family of proteins encoded by the hundreds of genes in the genome of the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
We have used this approach to identify and characterize a novel protein, MC179, which is expressed on the surface of Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells.
The degradation of Aux/IAA proteins is mediated by the SCF^TIR1/AFB complex, which is composed of an E2 enzyme and a substrate-specific E3 ubiquitin ligase.
We found that SOR1, an E3 ubiquitin ligase, negatively regulates auxin signaling in roots and positively regulates shoot growth.
We found that SOR1 is an E3 ligase that targets IAA26 for degradation and that OsIAA9 acts as a negative regulator of SOR1 to protect IAA26 from degradation.

This work reveals a previously unknown regulatory mechanism for auxin-mediated root growth inhibition and provides new insights into how plants integrate multiple hormone signals to regulate root development.
We found that loss of TAK1 in hepatocytes leads to increased hepatic inflammation, fibrosis, and cancer development.
We found that RIPK1 is required for liver injury in response to TAK1 deficiency and that this effect is mediated through the activation of NF-kB signaling.

We found that RIPK1 promotes liver fibrosis and hepatocarcinogenesis by regulating inflammatory responses through its kinase activity, which is independent of cell death or compensatory proliferation.
The mosquito Aedes aegypti is the primary vector for several important human pathogens including dengue, chikungunya, and Zika viruses.
We evaluated the effectiveness of an innovative, low-cost, and sustainable vector control strategy using three autocidal gravid ovitrap ( AGO trap) units per home in a rural Kenyan village.
Autoimmune disease is a major cause of morbidity in humans, but the genetic factors underlying these disorders are not well understood.

Genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) have identified many genetic variants associated with complex diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and Crohn's disease; however, most of these variants are located outside coding regions and do not affect gene expression levels.
We have previously shown that genetic variation in the CD24 gene is associated with multiple sclerosis ( MS), an autoimmune disease affecting the central nervous system.

We found that a single nucleotide change in the 3'-untranslated region ( 3'UTR) of CD24 mRNA, which is known to affect protein stability, confers protection from multiple sclerosis and systemic lupus erythematosus.

The use of oxytocin to treat labour dystocia (difficulty in vaginal delivery) has been shown to improve outcomes for both mother and baby, but it may also be associated with an increased risk of complications such as uterine rupture, which can result in serious injury or death for the mother and her baby.

Augmentation of labour for women with abnormal labour (dystocia) Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether augmentation of labour improves outcomes for mothers and babies when there are problems during labour.
Misoprostol is a drug that has been used for many years to induce labour in women who are at risk of having a premature birth (preterm birth).
Misoprostol is a prostaglandin analogue, which has been shown to induce labour in women at term when given vaginally or rectally.
The trials did not report on any maternal deaths, severe complications for the mother, or infant deaths.

Mg^2+ is an essential cofactor for protein synthesis, but its role in ribosome function has not been fully explored.
Here we show that low oxygen levels, high iron(II), and high manganese(II) can restore the activity of an inactive ribosome from a thermophilic organism to its active form.
These results provide evidence for the involvement of iron and manganese in the origin of life on Earth.
The concentration of carbonyl sulfide ( OCS) has been proposed to be a proxy for photosynthetic activity and plant water use efficiency.
We show that sulfur isotopes in OCS can be used as a tracer of its source, with implications for understanding the global sulfur budget.

The use of a semimetal as the channel material is also demonstrated for the first time to our knowledge in this work.
In this study we used machine learning methods to develop and evaluate a model for identifying dengue cases in patients presenting at an emergency department.
We found that the performance of all machine learning algorithms was significantly better than that of the traditional methods in predicting sepsis.
This study provides an alternative method for rapid diagnosis of dengue fever, which is especially useful in resource-poor settings.
The results show that the machine learning model is able to predict the outbreak of dengue fever in advance with high accuracy.

The immune system plays an important role in the resolution of inflammation after ischemia, but the mechanisms involved are not well understood.
Macrophage-derived growth factors promote angiogenesis and improve blood flow to ischemic tissues.

We show that RvD1 is increased in response to limb ischemia and that this increase promotes resolution of inflammation by promoting clearance of apoptotic cells and reducing recruitment of proinflammatory macrophages.

These findings provide insight into the mechanisms by which blood vessels are formed during wound healing and suggest that this process could be targeted therapeutically to improve outcomes in patients with impaired wound healing.
The low fertility rate of China has been a concern for decades, and it is expected to continue to decline in the coming years.
The assumption is that older people are less productive and more dependent on younger generations, which would lead to economic decline.
We find that the productivity-weighted labor force dependency ratio is a better predictor of future economic growth than the traditional dependency ratio.

This paper shows that the current population growth rate in China is not as high as it seems when measured by the total fertility rate and that the country's demographic transition has been faster than previously thought.
Sigma-1 receptors ( Sig-1Rs) are ligand-regulated ER-resident proteins that play critical roles in protein folding, lipid metabolism and cell survival.

We found that the current diagnostic tests are not very reliable and that they should be used with caution.
We did not find any significant difference in the frequency of clinical manifestations, severity and mortality rates among patients with paracoccidiodomycosis caused by P. brasiliensis and P. lutzii.
The results presented here are important for understanding the epidemiology and evolution of this disease in Brazil.
We find that climate has become increasingly important for explaining the spatial variability of tree-ring widths.

We show that climate change has already altered the synchrony between tree-ring growth and climate in Mediterranean forests, with implications for the interpretation of past climates from tree rings.
Water use efficiency of plants has increased globally since the beginning of the 20th century, but the magnitude and causes of this trend are not well understood.

We found that shrubs are more sensitive to drought than trees and that this difference is consistent with their contrasting leaf traits.
We find that the transition to more water-efficient vegetation is likely to have a net positive effect on plant health in most of the world's drylands.

We then develop a computational model of the chondriome and use it to predict the dynamics of mitochondrial DNA ( mtDNA) copy number across different tissues and cell types.
We show that the network exhibits a percolation transition, and we find that the transition is robust to changes in the number of mitochondria.

We propose that this intrinsic morphological instability of mitochondria may play an important role in cellular adaptation and evolution, and provide a new perspective on how cells optimize their functional competence through dynamic remodeling of the chondriome.

These results provide insight into how UvrD1 might sense oxidative stress and activate its function as an ATP-dependent DNA helicase.
Anticoagulants are drugs used to prevent blood clots from forming in the veins (venous thrombosis) or arteries (arterial thrombosis).
The formation and resolution of blood clots is an important process in maintaining normal circulation, but also plays a role in many diseases such as stroke and heart attack.
We have identified a new mechanism for how blood clots form in the presence of thrombin, which is a key enzyme involved in clotting.

We found that the fibrin cap limits platelet access to thrombin, thereby preventing platelet activation and limiting thrombus growth.
Fibrin is a protein that forms a protective barrier over blood clots to prevent them from growing and causing problems.

We also show that Mlh3 is required to maintain the stability and function of the meiotic recombination machinery, which is essential for proper chromosome segregation during meiosis.

Cooperation is essential to human societies, but it is difficult to explain why individuals cooperate with each other in the absence of direct benefits.

We show that in a group of humans playing an economic game, people's decisions are influenced by their own past experiences with others, but also by what they have heard about others from other players.
We find that even in environments with high error rates, cooperation is more likely to evolve than defection and that the probability of cooperation increases as the number of neighbors increases.

We found that these organisms are able to use light for growth by either using rhodopsin or conventional photosystem proteins.

Hormone replacement therapy for menopausal symptoms in women with premature ovarian failure Review question We reviewed the evidence on how well hormone replacement therapy (HRT) works to treat menopausal symptoms in women who have had an early menopause before the age of 40 years.
Hormone replacement therapy ( HRT) is often used to treat menopausal symptoms but it may also increase the risk of breast cancer and heart disease.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant studies published up until 12 June 2019.
All three trials compared HRT to no treatment.

Key results We found no clear evidence on whether HRT increases or decreases the risk of developing breast cancer, heart attack, stroke, transient ischaemic attack (TIA), or death from any cause in women with previous hysterectomy.
We did not find any information on the incidence of gallstones, which is an important outcome for people with diabetes mellitus.
We found no studies that looked at the effects of HRT on quality of life, bone mineral density, or risk of falls and fractures.

The Ag180 cage is a unique example of a protein structure that has been found to have a high degree of similarity with a mathematical object called a Platonic solid.
