The ATRX protein is a chromatin remodeling factor that has been implicated in regulating gene expression and genome stability.
We have used conditional knockout technology to study the function of Atrx during mouse embryogenesis.
We have found that the maternal copy of the gene is not expressed in the placenta and that the paternal copy is required for normal development of this organ.
We found that some female carriers were able to establish a normal placenta despite having two copies of the mutated gene.
These results provide new insights into the function of ATRX in mammalian development and suggest that ATRX may be a target for therapeutic intervention to treat human placental insufficiency.

Intracellular pathogens have evolved to target host cell signaling molecules such as Rabs and Arfs for their own benefit.
Chlamydiae are obligate intracellular bacteria that cause several human diseases and have evolved strategies to manipulate host cell trafficking pathways for their own benefit.

We found that Chlamydia infection induces expression of Rab6 and Rab11 in host cells, which are required for efficient nutrient uptake from the endolysosome system.
Our study provides new insights into how Chlamydia manipulates host cell trafficking pathways to promote infection.
We have previously shown that Streptococcus pneumoniae has evolved to produce a unique cell wall that is less immunogenic than other streptococcal species.
We found that the bacteria evolved to have a more stable peptidoglycan structure, which is likely to be important in maintaining the integrity of the bacterial envelope during infection.
We found that this change was caused by loss of a single gene, yacF, which is involved in the synthesis of peptidoglycan, a component of bacterial cell wall.
We also show that the loss of this gene results in an abnormal cell shape and that it is essential for the transition from cell elongation to cell division.
We found that the loss of this gene is accompanied by changes in the peptidoglycan structure and the loss of the elongation machinery.
We found that this gene has been lost in several species of Moraxella and Neisseria, but not in other related bacteria.
We show that this loss is associated with the release of previously suppressed genetic variation in the underlying color pattern genes, which may have facilitated the rapid evolution of new signals.
Our study provides new insights into how traits evolve over time and may help to explain why some species lose certain traits during their evolutionary history.

We show that the two populations have different light-harvesting properties and that only one of them is photosynthetically active.

Antibiotics for bacterial vaginosis in non-pregnant women Bacterial vaginosis (BV) is an infection of the vagina caused by bacteria, which affects about one-third of sexually active women at some time or another.
However, antibiotics are not always successful at treating infections and they have potential side effects.
We wanted to find out if using an intravaginal lactic acid gel was as good at curing BV as taking oral antibiotics.
The study compared two treatments, one using an antifungal cream and the other using an antibiotic.
We found that there was no difference between the two treatments in terms of the number of women who had a live birth.
We found that, compared to lactic acid gel, metronidazole is more likely to resolve symptoms of bacterial vaginosis within two weeks.
The study found no difference between the two treatments in terms of cure rate at 6 weeks or recurrence rates at 6 months.

Sponges are one of the oldest animal groups, with fossil records dating back to the Neoproterozoic Era ( ~1 billion y ago).
We found that sponges accumulate large amounts of polyphosphates ( polyP), which are known to be involved in many biological processes including cell signaling and regulation of gene expression.
We found that the polyphosphate granules are formed in response to oxidative stress, and they contain both phosphate and iron.
We found that many bacteria in sponges contain polyphosphate ( polyP) granules, which are known to be involved in bacterial survival and virulence.

We also found that the related adaptor protein AP-1 is required to maintain normal levels of regulated secretion and that it acts independently of AP-3.

We find that mice lacking Ten-m3 have severe visual impairments, including abnormal retinal wiring and impaired visual acuity.
We show that this deficit arises from an imbalance between the two eyes and not from a general reduction of visual acuity.
Our results suggest that Ten-m is an important regulator of axon guidance and synapse formation during visual system development.

This study provides evidence that the bacterial protein RbfA is a checkpoint factor that monitors the fidelity of 30S subunit assembly and prevents premature initiation of translation.
The global ocean is a net source of nitrogen gas ( N2) to the atmosphere, but the mechanisms responsible for this process are not well understood.
We show here that the deep ocean's most extreme environments, the hadal trenches, are hotspots for microbial life and biogeochemical cycling.

We show that the deep-sea trench environment is capable of supporting high rates of denitrification and anaerobic ammonium oxidation ( anammox), with the latter being the dominant pathway for nitrogen removal.

We show that these bacteria are likely to be piezotolerant, i.e., able to survive at high pressure, and that their activity is consistent with the presence of a large population of microorganisms in the deep ocean.

This study demonstrates that hadal sediments are biologically active environments with a functioning nitrogen cycle, which is likely driven by microorganisms living in close association with their hosts.

Bacillus Calmette-Guerin (BCG) is an intravesical therapy used to treat superficial bladder cancers after they have been removed by surgery.
The current treatment is BCG which has been used for over 100 years but it only works in about half of people.

Gemcitabine for advanced bladder cancer Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of gemcitabine in people with advanced bladder cancer to see if it was effective and safe.
We searched the literature to find all relevant studies and found one study with 40 patients who had recurrent superficial bladder cancer after previous treatment.
The review authors identified one small trial comparing a single dose of chemotherapy with a salt solution after surgery and found no clear evidence of any benefit for the chemotherapy.
Gemcitabine was also compared to mitomycin C, another drug used in BCG treatment, and found to be more effective at reducing tumour recurrence and progression.

Intravesical chemotherapy for non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer Review question We reviewed the evidence on how effective intravesical chemotherapy (chemotherapy given directly into the bladder) is at preventing recurrence or progression of non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC).
Gemcitabine is an anticancer drug that has been shown to be effective against bladder cancer cells grown in the laboratory and in animal models of bladder cancer.
Cloud seeding is an increasingly popular strategy for increasing precipitation in arid regions.

Glaciogenic seeding of clouds aims to increase snowfall by introducing ice-forming aerosols into clouds, but its effectiveness has been difficult to quantify due to the complexity of the underlying physical processes involved.
We show that the formation of ice particles in clouds is not as simple as previously thought and that the presence of supercooled water droplets is required for efficient ice formation.
We show that the ice crystals formed by glaciogenic seeding in clouds have a distinct size distribution, which is consistent with the expected response to the injection of large particles.
Liquid-liquid phase separation ( LLPS) is an emerging mechanism for compartmentalization and regulation of cellular processes.
We use a multiscale computational approach to study how the length of an RNA molecule affects its ability to form liquid-like condensates with RNA-binding proteins ( RBPs).
Our work provides new insights into how RNA binding proteins interact with their target RNAs and how this interaction affects the phase behavior of these proteins.

We show that the C-terminal tail of murine leukemia virus ( MLV) IN is required for binding to BET proteins, which are known to regulate gene expression by modulating chromatin structure.
We have developed a new MLV system with a disrupted integrase gene that is unable to integrate into DNA.

We found that mice infected with a virus lacking the TP gene had significantly longer survival times than those infected with WT virus, and that this was associated with reduced viral replication in the brain.

We found that while the rate of tumor formation was reduced in mice infected with MLV IN TP-, the virus still integrated near cancer-related genes and could be rescued by co-infection with wild-type virus.
Benznidazole is the only drug available for treating Chagas disease, but its efficacy and safety are still controversial.
In this study we used data from a large cohort of patients with chronic Chagas disease to evaluate whether or not previous treatment with benznidazole was associated with better outcomes.
We show that, in a tropical forest, most plants are visited by only one or two pollinators and that these visits are highly skewed toward a few dominant species.
We show that larger plants produce more seeds when they are surrounded by smaller plants, but this effect is absent in populations with high levels of genetic relatedness among individuals.
We show how the diversity of pollinator species contributes to the delivery of multiple ecosystem functions, including the maintenance of plant genetic diversity.

We found that in areas with ongoing transmission, the proportion of suspected cases with laboratory confirmation was higher than in outbreak settings ( 0-37% vs. 27-38%).

Genome wide association studies ( GWAS) have revealed thousands of genetic variants that are associated with disease risk and trait variation in humans.
The genetic variants are usually located near genes, and it is believed that they affect gene expression by altering the binding sites for transcription factors or other regulatory proteins.
This study highlights the importance of considering the contribution of rare variants when interpreting GWAS results.

We have developed a method for integrating multiple sources of information, including gene expression data from different tissues, protein-protein interaction networks, and regulatory network models, in order to identify new regulatory interactions.
However, these methods have limitations due to the presence of batch effects and other confounding factors in the original data.
We demonstrate the utility of our method by identifying novel regulators of Treg cell function and by predicting known regulators of Treg cell development.
APOBEC3G is an enzyme that mutates viral DNA and restricts HIV-1 replication in cells.
We show that the human APOBEC3G gene has been under positive selection in primates and that this selection is likely to be driven by HIV-1.
We show that APOBEC3G is an important factor in the evolution of HIV-1 and SIVmac, two viruses that are closely related but have different host species origins.

We show that rhesus macaques have multiple functional APOBEC3G variants, and that SIVmac evolved to evade this restriction by acquiring mutations in the viral genome that are known to be associated with resistance to APOBEC3G.
We found that the ability of SIVsmmVif to counteract rhesos APOBEC3G is due to specific amino acid changes in the C-terminal domain of SIVsmmVif.
Our study provides evidence that adaptation to a host species is an important driver of virus evolution and that this process may be ongoing during cross-species transmission events.

The Y chromosome has been used to identify the sex of an individual from ancient remains, but it has also been used in forensic science for many years to help identify individuals from mixed DNA samples.
However, it has been shown that the use of Y chromosome markers in forensic identification is not as reliable as the use of autosomal markers.
However, the increasing use of high-throughput sequencing has made it difficult for forensic scientists to convey the strength of evidence in court.
The probability of finding a DNA match between two individuals depends on the number of people in the population who are related to both individuals.
We find that the probability of a match is very low for most individuals and that the probability of multiple matches in a single sample is even lower.
Malaria is one of the most important causes of death and illness worldwide.
Malaria is a major public health problem in sub-Saharan Africa where it causes over one million deaths annually.
We developed a spatial model to map the distribution of malaria cases in Tanzania from 2000-2015.
We found that our approach is able to identify areas with high incidence of dengue, and also detect changes in disease patterns over time.

The results provide a framework for evaluating the effectiveness of interventions in different settings and will be useful to public health officials who need to design and implement effective dengue prevention and control programs.
Exposure therapy is an effective treatment for anxiety disorders; however, it is often difficult for patients to complete because of its aversive nature.

This study shows how exposure to fear-relevant stimuli in a virtual reality environment can be used as an alternative to traditional exposure therapy and may help overcome the limitations of current treatments.

We show that this is indeed the case for both visual and auditory stimuli, but that the representation remains stable only up to a certain number of environments ( i. e., 5-7).
We recorded neural activity from rats navigating through a virtual reality maze and found that the brain's internal representation of space is encoded by a population of neurons in the hippocampus.
We found that the number of independent spatial representations encoded by hippocampal neurons was surprisingly high and that these representations were highly specific to each environment.
Human cytomegalovirus ( HCMV) is an important pathogen for immunocompromised individuals, including transplant recipients and HIV-infected patients.

Our results provide insight into how the viral protein pUL89 might interact with DNA and facilitate genome packaging.

We find that melanoma, which has the highest mutation load, is evolving under negative selection, while other cancer types evolve under positive selection.
Our results show how the combination of these two factors can lead to a nonlinear effect on survival, which is consistent with clinical observations in cancer patients.

The mechanisms by which peripheral inflammation is sensed and communicated to the brain remain unclear.
We found that Cre reporter gene expression in neurons was due to the transfer of Cre mRNA from macrophages and microglia, which are brain-resident immune cells, into neurons.
In this study we show that Cre recombinase activity is increased in the brain of mice with experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis ( EAE), an animal model for multiple sclerosis ( MS).
Our findings provide evidence for an important role of extracellular RNAs as signaling molecules in the inflammatory response.
The introduction of invasive alien species is one of the most important threats to biodiversity worldwide.
We have compiled data on over 1, 300 invasive insects and pathogens in 124 countries around the world, and used this information to calculate the total economic impact of invasions for each country.

We show that countries differ greatly in terms of both risk and source status for invasive species, and that these differences are strongly related to country-specific characteristics such as climate, land use, and trade.
Muscle fibre arrangement is an important determinant of muscle function, but how it is established during development remains poorly understood.
The ability to measure muscle forces and lengths in vivo has been limited by the availability of suitable sensors.
Muscle and tendon are complex biological tissues with non-linear mechanical properties, which make them difficult to model using traditional engineering methods.
We have developed a computational model of the human lower limb musculoskeletal system which is able to simulate walking and running in a realistic manner.
We then used computer models to simulate how the muscle would behave under different conditions and found that the muscle is most likely to be stretched when the knee is bent at around 90 degrees.
We find that the muscle operates at an average length that is about 1.
We found that muscle fibres were not always aligned with the direction of movement and that the lengths of muscle fibres varied greatly between individuals.
This study provides new insights into the evolution of locomotion in tetrapods, and will aid future studies of fossilized trackways.
The cost-effectiveness of different interventions is difficult to assess because of the lack of data on the number of people affected by sleeping sickness and the number of cattle at risk.
We estimated the cost of using these methods in Uganda, and found that they would be more expensive than current standard practice but could potentially save lives.
The results show that the new approach is more cost-effective than previous approaches to tsetse control, and that it has the potential to be implemented at scale across Africa.
The use of small targets and local delivery systems has been shown to be effective in reducing the number of people who are bitten by mosquitoes and therefore reduce the risk of dengue fever.
In this study we show that mycorrhizal fungi ( MF) are able to sense nutrient availability in their environment and adjust their growth rate accordingly.
We use mathematical modeling and experimental data to show how mycorrhizal fungi trade phosphorus for carbon with their hosts in response to nutrient scarcity.

Our results provide evidence for a revealed preference hypothesis that is central to general equilibrium theory and have implications for how we should interpret experimental data on choice under uncertainty.

Botulinum toxin injections are often used to treat spasticity in people with stroke, but they need to be combined with other treatments such as physiotherapy and occupational therapy to help improve function.
The review found no clear evidence to support or reject the use of MD in the treatment of people with schizophrenia.
We wanted to find out which type of exercise programme is most likely to be effective in improving the quality of life for people with MS.
The review authors found 10 studies, involving 336 participants who had received botulinum toxin injections to treat upper limb spasticity after stroke.
We found no evidence that any of these treatments improved arm or hand function, or reduced spasticity (unusual stiffness) in the upper limb.
We did not find any evidence on the use of botulinum toxin injections to treat spasticity in children or people who have had surgery to remove part of their leg muscle (focal treatment).
There is some low quality evidence to suggest that multidisciplinary rehabilitation may improve arm movement after botulinum toxin injections, but more research is needed.
However, many people with long-term conditions are not able to use these services and so self-management programmes have been developed to help people manage their own condition at home.
We wanted to find out if these types of interventions are effective, acceptable and value for money.
We found 10 studies that evaluated the effects of self-management support on health outcomes in people with COPD.
We found 40 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of self-management support interventions for men with COPD.
We found 38 studies that explored how men experience and perceive self-management support.

We found that men's willingness to engage in support was influenced by their perceptions of what would be acceptable and expected of them as men, and how this might impact on their relationships with others.
We found that some elements of programmes were beneficial for men, but there was no evidence that they were better than other types of programme.
Infections with fungi are common in both immunocompetent and immunocompromised individuals, but the mechanisms of fungal virulence remain poorly understood.
Here we describe a new type of fungal recognition structure called the fungipod, which is induced in dendritic cells ( DC) upon contact with fungal cell wall components.
We show that Fungipods are formed from a dynamic network of actin filaments, which are stabilized by clathrin and cortactin.
We found that the mannose receptor CD206 is essential for this process and that it acts as a sensor of fungal cell wall components.
This study provides new insights into how fungal pathogens are recognized by the host and may lead to the development of novel therapeutics for invasive candidiasis.
We discovered that the bacterium Thermotoga maritima contains a unique set of genes for synthesizing 7-deazaadenosines, which are modified nucleobases found in some tRNAs.
We have renamed these genes as the 7-deazapurine in DNA ( dpd) gene cluster, and we show that they are essential for the synthesis of 7-deazapurines from guanine.
Early treatment with a plaster cast is better than delayed treatment for people who have had an acute (recent) wrist fracture.
The main reason for this is that X-rays are not always able to show whether or not there is a fracture.
This review aimed to find out which of these tests are best at identifying scaphoid fractures when X-rays do not show any evidence of a fracture.
The studies were divided into two groups, one group with studies that used a control group (the control group was not given any treatment) and one group with studies that did not use a control group.
We found no evidence of any difference in the number of deaths or serious adverse events (such as heart attacks and strokes) between people who had a CT scan and those who did not have a CT scan.
The results showed that ultrasound had the highest sensitivity, but lower specificity compared to computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).

The results of this study show that if we use computed tomography (CT) scans to identify which patients need surgery, then 2 out of every 10 people who have a CT scan would not need surgery and would be given unnecessary treatment.
The main drawback of this method is that it requires an invasive procedure and exposes patients to high levels of radiation.
The main limitation of this review was the low sensitivity of X-rays for detecting scaphoid fractures.
Retinopathy of prematurity ( ROP) is a disease of the retina, which affects babies born before term and who are exposed to supplemental oxygen during their first few months of life.
The review authors searched for studies in which babies were given vitamin A supplements and compared with babies who did not receive any supplements or received other types of supplements.

The review found no evidence to support this practice and concluded that there is insufficient evidence to recommend routine use of postoperative ventilation for preterm babies born at less than 32 weeks' gestation.
The review found no evidence to support the use of penicillamine for preventing retinopathy of prematurity in preterm babies.
Aging is associated with changes in metabolic pathways, which are thought to contribute to age-related diseases.
We found that the expression of metabolic genes is altered during aging, and that these changes are reflected by alterations in the levels of metabolites.
We find that glycine is the only amino acid whose concentration increases with age and that this increase is associated with reduced lifespan.

We found that the loss of the enzyme GlyA, which catalyzes the first step in glycine degradation, leads to an increase in glycine levels and a decrease in the expression of genes involved in glycine metabolism.
We find that glycine and serine supplementation have similar effects on the growth rate of yeast cells, suggesting that these amino acids may be functionally equivalent in this context.
These findings provide new insights into how dietary restriction may affect longevity and healthspan.
We use molecular dynamics simulations to study the binding-induced folding of an intrinsically disordered protein fragment ( IDP), which is known to block the active site of a protease.
We find that the native-like interactions are not sufficient for the initial binding and that non-native interactions play an essential role in this process.
This study provides insight into the mechanism of action of an important class of drugs and will be useful for the rational design of new inhibitors.
The parvoviruses are small viruses that infect many different species and cause disease in humans, livestock and wildlife.
Here, we report that the viral protein L1 is responsible for recruiting the cellular ubiquitination machinery to the replication center and promoting the degradation of the host cell protein p21.
We found that p21 is targeted to the virus replication center by binding to PCNA and that this interaction is necessary for recruitment of the E3 ubiquitin ligase CRL4Cdt2 to the replication center.
Viruses have evolved multiple strategies to evade host immunity, including encapsidation of viral genomes in protein shells ( virions).
Here we show that TRIM5a is not required to protect cells from HIV-1 infection in the presence of antibodies targeting the virus envelope protein gp120.
We show that TRIM21 is required for efficient recognition of both DNA and RNA viruses in vivo.
We show that this mechanism is essential for the rapid induction of an immune response to virus infection, which is critical for protection from disease.
Clear cell renal cell carcinoma ( ccRCC) is one of the most common forms of kidney cancer.
We found that the prefoldin complex is required to maintain the stability of VHL and its function as an E3 ubiquitin ligase.
Here, we show that prefoldin interacts with VHL and modulates its stability in vitro and in vivo.
This study provides new insights into how the prefoldin complex functions in protein folding, which may have implications for understanding the pathogenesis of von Hippel-Lindau disease.
The study of convergent evolution has been hampered by the lack of a method to identify homologous genes across different species.
However, it is unclear how often this occurs in nature and whether it has an impact on our ability to infer species relationships.
We show how to calculate the probability of homoplasy in a phylogenetic tree given the distribution of traits on the tree.
The frontopolar cortex ( FPC), located at the very front of the brain, has been implicated in many cognitive functions including decision making, planning, and social cognition.
However, the function of this structure is not well understood.
We found that FPC lesions impair rapid object recognition in both familiar and unfamiliar contexts, but not in familiar scenes or when subjects are given more time to learn.
Our results show that the frontal pole cortex is critical for guiding behavior in situations where animals must rapidly learn about the relative value of different behavioral options.
The number of essential genes is unknown in most organisms, but it is known to be small in some bacteria and yeast.
We have carried out a large-scale genetic screen for mutations affecting the development of the inner ear using the mouse as a model system.
We found that the number of essential genes is higher in regions with lower recombination rates, and that this relationship holds true for both human and mouse.
p53 is a central regulator of cell survival, proliferation, and apoptosis.
p53 is one of the most important tumor suppressor genes in human cancers and its function is regulated by a large number of post-transcriptional regulators including miRNAs.
However, the mechanisms by which these miRNAs regulate p53 are not well understood.
We also demonstrate how this model can be used to predict the effect of microRNAs on the dynamics of p53 in response to DNA damage.
We show that this positive feedback loop is essential for the robustness of the p53 network and its ability to respond to different types of DNA damage.
Liquid crystals are ubiquitous in modern life, from smartphones to televisions and computer monitors.
We found that indoor air quality is significantly affected by the emissions from electronic devices such as liquid crystal display ( LCD) screens and televisions.
This study demonstrates that LCD screens are an important source of indoor air pollution and should be included in future studies to better understand the sources of indoor air pollutants.
We found that mosquito diversity was higher in forests and lower in developed areas, but that this pattern was not consistent across all species.

We found that avian viruses tend to be transmitted by a small number of mosquito species that are specialized for transmission of these viruses, whereas mammalian viruses are transmitted by a broad range of mosquito species.

Our results highlight the importance of considering the spatial heterogeneity in mosquito abundance when designing surveillance programs to detect dengue virus transmission, and provide insights into how this heterogeneity may be used to improve the efficiency of these programs.
Breath-holding attacks are episodes of apnea (temporary cessation of breathing) and cyanosis (bluish discoloration of the skin).
Iron deficiency is thought to be one of the causes of breath-holding attacks, but there are no studies examining this association directly.
The review found that iron supplementation may reduce the number of episodes, but not their duration or severity.
Breast milk is considered to be the best food for infants and provides them with essential nutrients and antibodies against many pathogens.
Our results suggest that breastfeeding should be avoided in case of USUV infection as well.
We develop an approach to estimate the sexual orientation of individuals in the United States using mortgage lending records.

We find that neighborhoods with higher shares of same-sex couples have less access to credit, but this effect is driven by the fact that these neighborhoods also have higher shares of single men who are less likely to be able to obtain credit.
The inositol phospholipid PIP2 is an important second messenger in cells.
We have developed a method for identifying proteins that bind to inositol phosphates ( InsPs) and pyrophosphoinositides ( PP-InsPs).
We found that the majority of these proteins are involved in metabolic processes such as glycolysis, ribosome biogenesis, and protein synthesis.
This work demonstrates that inositol phosphates are important regulators of cellular metabolism and that they may be targeted for therapeutic intervention.
We identify the major drivers of mangrove loss globally and show that the expansion of rice cultivation in Myanmar is an important but previously unrecognised threat.
We show that mangroves are being cleared at an alarming rate across Southeast Asia, with the highest rates occurring in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Myanmar.

Neutrophils have been shown to play an important role in the control of bacterial infections and inflammation; however, their function in the context of viral infection has not yet been fully explored.
We found that neutrophils from preeclamptic women are unable to transfer FOXO1 to T cells, which is essential for the induction of niT cells.
Our study provides evidence for an important role of neutrophils in regulating angiogenesis and trophoblast invasion, which may be relevant to human pregnancy complications such as preeclampsia.
Dendritic cells ( DCs) are specialized antigen-presenting cells that recognize pathogens via pattern recognition receptors such as Toll-like receptors ( TLRs).
The innate immune system is activated by pathogen recognition receptors, which trigger the production of type I interferons ( IFNs).
We have previously shown that the type I interferons ( IFN-I) produced by DCs are important for their ability to activate CD8+ T cells.
The photosynthetic electron transport chain is vulnerable to oxidative damage, and plants have evolved mechanisms to prevent this damage.
We report here that the photosynthetic protein ferredoxin-NADP reductase ( FNR) from cyanobacteria uses vibrational coupling between electronic states to protect against photooxidation.
We show that this mechanism is responsible for the observed temperature dependence of the excitonic absorption spectrum and the efficiency of exciton quenching in these materials.
The vaccine candidate was safe and immunogenic in humans and protected against chikungunya fever in non-human primate models.
This study provides evidence that the chimeric virus is safe and immunogenic in mice, making it an ideal candidate for future clinical evaluation.
The COVID-19 pandemic has had a profound impact on the global economy and society.
In response to COVID-19, governments have implemented policies such as lockdowns to reduce transmission of the virus.
We found that people's thoughts about COVID-19 were more negative when they had less time for socializing, but not when they worked from home.

We show that the contact angle of water on a solid surface depends strongly on the local geometry of the surface, and we demonstrate how this dependence can be used to quantify the surface roughness of porous materials.

This work provides insight into how natural materials have evolved to achieve optimal wetting properties for their function and suggests that similar strategies could be applied in engineering applications.
Segmental duplications ( SDs) are large DNA segments that have been copied from one location to another within the same genome, or between different genomes.
The mechanisms underlying this phenomenon are still unknown and represent an important question in the field of neuroscience.

We found that in budding yeast, replication stress is a major cause for the formation of single-stranded DNA ( ssDNA) gaps and that these gaps are converted to double-strand breaks by the action of the Mre11-Rad50-Xrs2 complex.
We show that the majority of these deletions arise from template switching events, which involve recombination between homologous sequences in different copies of the same gene.

We show how this mechanism explains many features of mammalian genomes, including the high frequency of transposable element insertions in splice sites, and the origin of some cancer-causing gene fusions.

We found that N. profundicola has evolved several unique features, including an expanded repertoire of genes for sulfur metabolism, which are likely important for its ability to thrive in the sulfidic environments of the deep ocean.
We found that this organism has a unique combination of enzymes that allow it to grow at high temperatures and in low oxygen conditions.
We found that RGY is induced by heat shock, and that it is conserved among bacteria living at high temperatures.

We show that N. profundicola has all of the genetic information required to survive in an anaerobic environment rich in hydrogen gas and carbon dioxide, which is thought to have been present on Earth during the Archean era.
Biodiversity is essential to human well-being, but it has been declining at an alarming rate.
Biodiversity loss is accelerating globally, but its effects on human health are not yet fully understood.
We show that marine food systems in tropical coastal areas are more likely to provide essential nutrients when they include many different species with diverse functions.
We show how biodiversity-ecosystem functioning research can be used to identify key ecosystem services that are most likely to benefit from conservation efforts.
The aim of this review was to find out if lowering blood pressure in people with acute stroke improves outcomes and reduces death.
The aim of this review was to find out whether or not lowering blood pressure is beneficial for patients who have had a stroke.
We found no evidence for any benefit from using antihypertensive drugs to lower blood pressure in people who have had an acute stroke.
We found no studies of people with dementia who were living in care homes, or of those who had received a diagnosis of dementia but did not have any symptoms.
Study characteristics We found 26 studies with 17,011 participants.
We found no evidence of benefit for any particular drug class in this setting.
More research is needed to find out if it would be better to start treatment earlier and who might benefit more than others.
The ability to generate variability in gene expression has been shown to be important for proper development and function of many tissues, including the brain.
We have studied how cells fold to form a sphere in the green alga Volvox carteri.
Our results provide evidence for a transition from simple to complex multicellularity in the evolution of green algae, which is likely to be applicable to other organisms as well.
Crohn's disease ( CD) is an inflammatory bowel disease characterized by severe inflammation of the gastrointestinal tract.
We found that individuals with celiac disease are at increased risk of developing type 1 diabetes ( T1D) compared to the general population.
We found that CD and celiac disease ( CD) are associated with similar genetic variants, suggesting that they may be caused by similar mechanisms.

We found that several of these genes are associated with both diseases, including IL18RAP and PTPN2, which were previously known to be associated with type 1 diabetes ( T1D) and rheumatoid arthritis ( RA), respectively.

We performed a meta-analysis of GWAS data from Crohn's disease ( CD) and celiac disease ( CelD), two common gastrointestinal disorders with similar genetic backgrounds, to identify shared susceptibility genes.
We also identified seven new risk variants for Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, which we validated in an independent cohort of patients with inflammatory bowel diseases.
We identified several new genetic variants associated with CD and/or Crohn's disease ( CD), including two novel loci on chromosome 6q21 and 10p13.
We found that there are four genes ( PTPN2, IL18RAP, TAGAP, and PUS10) that were associated with both Crohn's disease and celiac disease.
The use of live attenuated vaccines ( LAVs) has been a cornerstone of vaccination for many infectious diseases including influenza virus infections.
Eastern equine encephalitis virus ( EEEV) is a mosquito-borne pathogen that causes severe disease in humans and horses.
There are currently no approved treatments available to treat HCV infection and it remains one of the most important causes of liver disease worldwide.
We have used this system to identify four genes that are required for EEEV replication and pathogenicity, and we show that these genes are not essential for virus growth in cell culture.
We found that these mutants are highly attenuated in vivo and do not cause disease in mice.
We found that all of these vaccines protect mice from subcutaneous infection and one vaccine protects mice from aerosol exposure to EEEV.

This study demonstrates that it is possible to produce live attenuated vaccines from highly pathogenic viruses by identifying and removing virulence factors, and that these vaccines are effective in protecting against lethal challenge.
Filovirus infections cause severe disease in humans and non-human primates.
However, the mechanism by which filoviruses are released from infected cells remains unclear.
We show that budding occurs through an unusual mechanism in which the viral genome is first associated with the cell surface and then enveloped by the host membrane.
The mechanism by which this occurs is not well understood, but it has been proposed to involve the formation of a lipidic pore through which the viral genome escapes from the endosome.
The activation of TRP channels is regulated by a variety of stimuli, including temperature and mechanical forces.
We used a previously developed Markovian model to analyze the probability flux through the channel in response to different stimuli.

We show how the transition probabilities and rates can be used to predict the response of the receptor to different ligands, and we use this approach to identify the key steps in the activation mechanism.
Dengue fever is a mosquito-borne disease caused by dengue virus ( DENV) infection, which has become one of the most prevalent infectious diseases in tropical and subtropical regions worldwide.
Dengue is endemic in Indonesia and has been responsible for thousands of deaths annually since 2000.
We found that the risk of dengue infection is higher among people with lower income, less education, and those who are unemployed or work as informal workers.
We found that dengue cases were more common in households with lower socioeconomic status and among people who worked outdoors.
Therefore, we suggest that preventive measures should be implemented in these settings to prevent dengue transmission.

This study provides an insight to the spatial distribution of dengue fever in the city of Manaus, Amazonas, Brazil, and may be useful for public health authorities to implement control strategies against dengue fever in other cities with similar environmental characteristics.
The electrical conductivity of most materials increases with increasing pressure due to the increased overlap between neighboring atoms and molecules.
Infections with intestinal worms ( helminths) are common in many parts of the world, especially in low-income countries.
However, the molecular mechanisms underlying these changes are not well understood.
We have shown that schistosome infection is associated with reduced levels of inflammatory mediators in urine, which may be important for reducing allergic responses to allergens.

We found that S. haematobium infection is associated with reduced levels of IgE antibodies to common allergens such as Ascaris lumbricoides and hookworms, and also with reduced levels of TLR2 and SOCS-3 gene expression.

Zika virus ( ZIKV) is an emerging mosquito-borne flavivirus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes and has been associated with severe neurological complications in adults and congenital abnormalities in newborns.

This study provides evidence of the potential role of Aedes albopictus as an alternative vector of Zika virus in French Polynesia and highlights the need to consider this mosquito species in the context of future outbreaks.
Genome-wide functional genomic studies have been used to identify genes and pathways involved in many biological processes, including cell cycle regulation.
However, the integration of high-throughput data from different sources and experimental conditions remains challenging.

We have developed an algorithm, called IMPACT ( Interaction-based Phenotype Analysis and Clustering Tool), which identifies groups of genes with similar phenotypes by analyzing their interactions in the context of the entire network.
We use the information about gene interactions to filter out phenotypes that are not relevant for the analysis and to identify new phenotypes that are associated with specific biological processes.

We applied this method to an RNA interference ( RNAi) screen for genes involved in endocytosis and found that it significantly increased the number of hits with consistent phenotypes compared to standard methods.

We demonstrate how IMPACT can be used to select high-confidence gene lists from large-scale functional screens and provide an example of its application in identifying novel regulators of cell cycle progression.
We show how this can be done in a simple way, and we demonstrate its effectiveness using simulations and experiments with human participants.
Here, we show that people's eye movements are sensitive to the moral content of sentences and that this sensitivity is not due to general attentional factors.
We show how this can be achieved by using a combination of behavioral experiments and computational modeling.

We present a method for characterizing the mechanical properties of membranes using micropipette aspiration, which is widely used in cell mechanics studies but has not been applied to lipid bilayer systems.
We find that the tension in the membrane is not uniform and varies with distance from the center of the patch.
This work has important implications for the interpretation of single-channel recordings from other types of ion channels.
Adaptive management is an approach to managing natural resources in the face of uncertainty, but it has been criticized for being too slow or ineffective.
We present an evaluation of the long-term effectiveness of community-based marine protected areas ( MPAs) in Papua New Guinea.

We show how an adaptive management system, based on rotational closures, can be used to improve the sustainability of a tropical marine fishery by increasing the abundance of target species and making them more vulnerable to fishing.
We developed PCR-Restriction fragment length polymorphism ( RFLP) assays to identify Leishmania species, which are based on the analysis of DNA fragments generated by restriction enzymes.
T cells are central components of the adaptive immune system and rely on their antigen-specific receptor for pathogen recognition.
We use data from high-throughput sequencing to study how the immune system generates antibodies in response to infection.
We show that this is due to the fact that a chains recombine on both chromosomes and that the two recombinations are correlated.
We find that the probability to generate an antibody with a given sequence is very low, and that the number of antibodies generated in the body is much larger than previously thought.
Our results provide a proof-of-concept for targeting virus egress with small molecules, which may be used in combination with other anti-HCMV therapies to improve treatment outcomes.
Our results provide a foundation for the development of novel antiviral therapies to treat HSV-1, HSV-2, and other viruses that replicate in response to cellular stress.
We have used a mouse model of acute infection with the Friend retrovirus to study how the immune system responds to viral infection.
We found that this approach led to an increase in the number of virus-specific CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, which was associated with reduced viremia.
We found that melanoma patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors (CIT) had increased levels of circulating cytotoxic T cells, which were associated with improved survival.
We found that acute infections are associated with severe disease manifestations in patients receiving corticosteroids, suggesting that effective measures to prevent infections should be implemented.

The molecular identity of the nociceptive and pruritic neurons in the spinal cord has been studied extensively; however, the molecular signature of the neurons that transmit these signals to the brain remains unknown.
We used a novel method to identify and characterize the molecular properties of these cells in vivo.
We show that these two sensations are processed by distinct populations of nociceptive sensory neurons, which have different molecular profiles and innervate different parts of the body.
We found 11 studies that looked at how well spinal nerve root blocks worked in diagnosing nerve root compression.

The review authors found that there is no evidence to support the use of routine screening for abdominal masses in children, but they were unable to draw any conclusions about whether or not it should be done.
Study characteristics We found 19 studies with a total of 1945 participants.
The results of this review show that there is no evidence to support the use of any particular test for diagnosing these conditions, and that combining tests does not improve diagnostic accuracy.
The brain is capable of reorganizing itself through changes in the strength of connections between neurons, as well as the addition or removal of synapses.
Here, we show that the amyloid precursor protein ( APP) is required for axon elimination during development of the Drosophila visual system.
Testicular germ cell tumors ( TGCT) are the most common malignancy in young men and have a high cure rate when diagnosed at an early stage.

We have identified a zebrafish mutant line that is highly prone to develop testicular cancer and show that this phenotype is caused by a mutation in lrrc50, which encodes a protein involved in cilia structure and function.
We found that the majority of zebrafish tumors have lost one copy of the normal gene, and that this loss is associated with increased tumor growth.
We found that loss-of-function mutations in LRRC50 are associated with human testicular cancer, including seminoma, and that these mutations reduce protein levels of the wild-type gene product.
Our results suggest that loss of Lrrc50 may contribute to the development of human seminomas.
The diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder ( ASD) relies on clinical judgment, which is highly variable between clinicians.

We show that autistic individuals are not impaired in their ability to use social cues to infer the stakes of social interactions but instead have difficulty using this information to guide their behavior.
We found that only the non-competitive participants were able to adjust their strategy based on the knowledge of others' incentives.

We found that autistic individuals have reduced ability to infer others' intentions from their actions in social games, which is associated with reduced activation of brain regions involved in theory-of-mind reasoning.
Our results suggest that the current nosology of autism spectrum disorders should be revised to include these subtypes, which could guide more effective treatment strategies.
Proteins are often found in complex with other proteins to form macromolecular assemblies that perform specific functions within cells.
We have used computer simulation to study how two proteins interact with each other at an atomic level.
We found that when two proteins make contact at nonnative interfaces, they often disassociate before reaching their native conformational ensemble.
Methotrexate is now widely used as the initial treatment for people with this disease and it is not clear whether or not radiotherapy should be added to methotrexate.
The aim of this review was to assess the efficacy and safety of chemotherapy (alone or combined with radiotherapy) for the treatment of newly diagnosed NPC patients.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to February 2014.
One study compared patients who received methotrexate and then had WBR to those who received methotrexate alone.
We found that there was no difference in the number of people who died or had serious complications between the two groups.
We found that there is insufficient evidence to show whether or not whole brain radiation improves overall survival in patients with newly diagnosed GBM, and more research is needed.
The results of this review suggest that adding whole brain radiotherapy to chemotherapy may improve overall survival compared to chemotherapy alone for people with newly diagnosed GBM.
Neurotoxicity was reported in 49% of patients who received both chemotherapy and radiotherapy and in 26% of patients who received chemotherapy alone.
Valosin-containing protein ( VCP) is an essential protein that plays important roles in multiple cellular functions including protein degradation, organelle biogenesis, and cell division.
We found that mutations in TER94 cause severe defects in muscle and nerve tissues, which are consistent with the clinical features observed in IBMPFD patients.
We found that mutations in the gene ter94, which encodes an AAA+ ATPase, cause defects in mitochondrial function and lead to increased levels of reactive oxygen species ( ROS).
Our results provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying this disease and suggest that alterations in cellular energy metabolism may be a common feature of IBMPFD.
The chromosome organization of bacteria is highly conserved, even between species with different lifestyles and environments.
Here we show that the E. coli genome is organized into large-scale domains ( termed"macrodomains") that are replicated at different times during the cell cycle.
We find that changes in the composition and expression of genes within these regions have little impact on cell physiology.

We show that this is due to the fact that the recombination machinery is not able to efficiently repair double-strand breaks in the shorter arm, which are generated by the imbalance in replication rates.
We found that the Ter region is required for proper chromosome segregation and that its disruption leads to abnormal chromosome segregation in mitosis.
We discuss how this may affect the regulation of the bacterial cell cycle, and how it might have contributed to the evolution of bacterial chromosomes.
We show here that microtubules are present in the bacterium Caulobacter crescentus, which is closely related to the last common ancestor of all eukaryotes.
Here, we report on the discovery of bacterial microtubules ( bMTs) in Myxococcus xanthus.
The bMT is unique among microtubules because it has only five protofilaments and lacks the conserved tubulin dimer interface.
We show that these proteins are not related to modern tubulins but instead likely evolved from an ancient form of tubulin that was involved in the formation of short-lived microtubules.
This study provides new insights into how HIV-1 infects resting CD4+ T cells and may help explain why these cells are not productively infected by HIV-1.
We have identified several cellular factors that interact with HIV-1 RNA and are required for its nuclear retention.

We found that HIV-infected CD4+ T cells from patients on long-term antiretroviral therapy ( HAART) are not fully protected against viral reactivation and that this is due to an increased expression of the host protein phosphatase 2A ( PP2A).
This study provides insight into how HIV-1 latency is maintained and how it might be reversed.
The ability of fungi to degrade complex organic compounds is essential for their survival in nature.
However, it remains unclear how these two types of genes have evolved in fungi.
We found that gene duplication is the major driver of fungal metabolic diversification, while horizontal transfer is rare and occurs mainly between closely related species.
The brain's ability to recognize objects and scenes is thought to be mediated by specialized modules of neurons in the ventral visual pathway.
We show that this category selectivity is not due to low-level visual features but rather reflects a more abstract representation of object shape and size.

We demonstrate the utility of our method on several datasets from different organisms and experimental systems.
We show that this approach is capable of identifying novel target genes for miRNAs and lncRNAs, as well as uncovering new regulatory interactions between these two classes of non-coding RNAs.

Phenytoin for preventing epilepsy after traumatic brain injury Review question We reviewed the evidence from randomised controlled trials to see if antiepileptic drugs, specifically phenytoin, prevent epilepsy after head injury.

We found no evidence to support the use of any specific treatment for this condition, but we did find some evidence that using a combination of treatments may be more effective than using one single treatment alone.

Antiepileptic drugs versus placebo, usual care or other medications for preventing epilepsy after traumatic brain injury Review question We reviewed the evidence on how well different medicines work to prevent epilepsy in people who have had a head injury.
We found no evidence to support the use of antiepileptic drugs for people with TBI who have had an early seizure, or those who are at risk of having an early seizure.
We found no significant difference in the risk of seizures or death from any cause between those who received phenytoin and those who did not receive phenytoin.
The review found no evidence for an effect on the risk of death, and there were concerns about the reliability of the information available from some of the studies.
We found no evidence on how well these drugs work in terms of reducing seizures and improving quality of life.

Animals must partition their environment to survive; they do so along three dimensions: space, time, and resources.
The effects of human activity on animal populations are well documented, but the extent to which we alter the way animals use resources remains unknown.
Here we use stable isotopes to examine how human activities alter the trophic structure of mammalian carnivore communities across North America.

Our results show that human disturbance alters the trophic structure of mammalian communities by increasing the consumption of human food subsidies and altering the trophic niches of both mesocarnivores and apex predators.
We found that human disturbance to wild prey species increases the risk of human-carnivore conflict and interspecific competition for resources between sympatric predators.
Antimicrobial stewardship programmes (AMSPs) are interventions designed to reduce the use of antibiotics in hospitals by improving how they are prescribed.
We wanted to find out more about what happens when doctors are given feedback on their antibiotic prescribing and whether or not this changes their behaviour.
We found no studies on how to improve antibiotic prescribing by doctors in training.
We found no studies of how to improve the quality of care for people with dementia and their carers in community settings.

We found that there are many ways to improve antibiotic prescribing by doctors in training, but these need to take account of the complex relationships between doctors and other staff within hospitals.

We found that doctors' confidence and knowledge about prescribing were not necessarily related, and that doctors who had more experience with prescribing felt less confident than those who had less experience.

The presence of arboranes in ancient rocks has been interpreted as evidence for the existence of terrestrial plants prior to their first appearance in the fossil record.

Our results provide new insights into the evolution of triterpenoid biosynthesis, and we have developed a method for distinguishing between sterol and arborinol synthase genes in genomic and metagenomic data.
Aspergillosis is caused by fungi belonging to the genus Aspergillus and is a major cause of morbidity and mortality in immunocompromised patients.
The fungus Aspergillus fumigatus produces a polysaccharide called galactosaminogalactan ( GAG) that is essential for its ability to infect humans.
We also show that less virulent Aspergillus species have reduced levels of galactosamine-containing glycosaminoglycan ( GAG) in their cell walls, which may contribute to their reduced virulence.
We found that increasing the galactosamine content of Aspergillus cell walls enhances their ability to adhere to lung epithelial cells and increases virulence in immunocompromised mice.

Insect molting is a complex process involving multiple physiological changes, including the synthesis of new cuticle components such as chitin and proteins.

We identified that miR-71 and miR-263 regulate chitin synthesis and degradation through targeting chitin synthase ( CHS) and chitinase ( CHT), respectively.
We found that miR-71 and miR-263 target two chitin synthases ( CHS1 and CHT10) to regulate chitin synthesis in locusts.
We found that miRNAs are involved in regulating chitin synthesis during molting.

Our study demonstrates that host phylogeny is not a major determinant of gut microbial composition in mammals, but rather that the spatial distribution of bacteria within their habitats is the primary driver of community assembly.

The aim of this review was to assess whether or not there are any benefits for people with dementia who receive care at home compared to those receiving care in an institution.

The review found no evidence to support the use of antibiotics for preventing or treating acute exacerbations in people with cystic fibrosis.

Streptococcus pneumoniae ( pneumococcus) is a major human pathogen causing pneumonia, meningitis, bacteremia, and sepsis.
We found that S. pneumoniae causes more severe disease in older mice than younger mice, which was associated with increased bacterial burden and exaggerated inflammation.
We found that macrophages from older mice are more susceptible to S. pneumoniae than those from younger mice and that this increased susceptibility is due to reduced autophagy.
Here, we show that Streptococcus pneumoniae induces autophagy in mouse macrophages through a mechanism involving bacterial phospholipase A2 ( PLY).
We found that macrophages from aged mice are defective in phagocytosis and bacterial clearance, and produce more inflammatory mediators than those from young mice.
We found that inhibition of LAP, which is produced by macrophages, enhances bacterial survival and inflammatory responses to S. pneumoniae in young but not aged mice.

This study demonstrates that macrophages are capable of producing lysozyme in response to pneumococcus and that this innate immune defense mechanism declines with age, which may be a factor contributing to increased risk for invasive pneumococcal disease in older adults.

However, it is unclear whether or not they are harmful to humans.

Epstein-Barr virus ( EBV) is a ubiquitous herpesvirus that infects most humans by age 5 years, but it remains latent in memory B cells for life.
Latent membrane protein 2A ( LMP2A) is an Epstein-Barr virus ( EBV) oncogene that mimics the function of the cellular B-cell receptor ( BCR).
However, the molecular mechanisms by which LMP2A promotes tumor progression are not fully understood.
We used proteomic analysis to identify proteins affected by LMP2A expression in EBV-infected B cells.

We found that LMP2A affects the expression of several proteins involved in cell cycle regulation, including the tumor suppressor retinoblastoma-associated protein 1 ( RB1) and the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2.
These findings provide new insights into how EBV infection of B lymphocytes leads to cancer development.

The DNA polymerases are enzymes responsible for replicating and repairing damaged DNA.
Mutations in DNA polymerase delta ( Pol d) are associated with several human diseases, including immunodeficiency and cancer.
In this study we show that loss of the proofreading function of DNA polymerase delta ( Pol d) leads to increased genomic instability and cancer formation in Drosophila melanogaster.
Fear is an adaptive response to danger, but in anxiety disorders it becomes maladaptive and interferes with daily life.

We show that the ventral hippocampus encodes information about the context of fear conditioning and inhibits the expression of fear responses when it is encountered again.

We found that ventral hippocampus neurons projecting to the prelimbic cortex are activated by both safety and compound cues, whereas those projecting to the infralimbic cortex or basolateral amygdala are only activated by threat cues.

The innate immune system is an essential component of our defense against pathogens and other harmful agents.
The innate immune system is responsible for detecting and eliminating pathogens from the body.
The innate immune response is critical for host defense against infection and injury, but how it responds to multiple insults simultaneously is poorly understood.
We have previously shown that an influenza virus infection in mice causes delayed wound healing and we now show that this is due to the immune response mounted by the host against the virus.
We found that the immune system responds more strongly to an infection than to a wound and that this response is not affected by the presence of a wound.
We show that the immune response to infection is not always linear and that the immune system prioritizes responses to different pathogens based on their relative threat.
Our results suggest that the innate immune system has evolved to handle multiple stimuli in a way that minimizes the negative consequences of cross-talk between different signaling pathways.

The primary bile acids ( PBAs), synthesized by the liver, play an essential role in lipid digestion and absorption.
The gut microbiota is known to metabolize bile acids ( BAs) produced in the liver, but the metabolic products formed during this process have not been fully characterized.
The bacterial bile acid hydrolase ( BSH) is a key enzyme in this process.

We found that the ability of BSH to detoxify bile acids is not sufficient to explain their toxicity in vitro; rather, it is the intrinsic chemical properties of individual bile acids that are responsible for their toxicity.
This work provides new insights into how gut bacteria sense bile acids, which may be useful for engineering probiotics or designing new drugs to treat metabolic disorders.

Bacillus anthracis, the causative agent of anthrax, is a spore-forming bacterium that infects many animals including humans.
The disease is caused by Bacillus anthracis and has been reported to be endemic in many countries around the world.
We found that the disease is still present in the region, with a high incidence rate and a significant number of cases reported in the last decade.
We found that the risk of human cutaneous anthrax was higher in rural areas, but also in urban areas where people are more likely to be exposed to contaminated soil.
This information will help to identify high-risk areas, which can then be targeted with interventions to reduce the incidence of brucellosis in cattle.
Biosurfactant lipopeptides are produced by bacteria, fungi, and plants and have been used as antimicrobial agents for decades.
Nonribosomal peptides ( NRP) are produced by nonribosomal peptide synthetases ( NRPS), which are large protein complexes that synthesize these molecules from amino acids and other building blocks.
We found that these compounds are produced by bacteria from the genus Streptomyces, which are known for their ability to produce secondary metabolites with diverse biological activities.
This work highlights the potential of ribosome-based biocatalysis as an alternative route toward the production of modified peptides with unique properties.
Neural crest cells are a transient population of stem cells that migrate extensively during embryonic development, giving rise to many different cell types in the head and trunk.

We found that miR-146a and miR-155 are upregulated in human macrophages infected by dengue virus, while miR-21 is down-regulated.

Xylella fastidiosa is a bacterial pathogen that colonizes the xylem and causes Pierce's disease in grapevines and leaf scorch on citrus trees.
This study provides evidence that X. fastidiosa uses OMV production as a means to modulate cell-cell interactions and to facilitate colonization of plants.
This study provides insight into how bacteria move through porous materials and how they might be able to survive in these environments.

The World Color Survey ( WCS), which has collected color naming data from over 1,000 speakers of 110 languages, shows that all languages have better color terms for yellows and reds than blues and greens.
This result suggests that color naming is not only driven by universal factors, but also by universal principles governing color use.
We found that speakers of languages with more useful colors had better color memory, and that this relationship was mediated by attentional resources.
We found that Tsimane9 have more difficulty naming colors of natural objects than artificial ones, and that this difficulty is associated with their limited exposure to artificial materials.
Omecamtiv mecarbil is a small molecule inhibitor of cardiac myosin ATPase that has been shown to increase cardiac contractility in animal models and humans.

Our results suggest that the molecular mechanism of force generation in muscle is more complex than previously thought.
Telomeres are protective caps on chromosome ends that prevent them from being recognized as damaged DNA by the cell's own surveillance mechanisms.
We show that yeast cells lacking telomerase are unable to survive when they lack Rad52, a protein involved in most types of DNA repair.
Here, we show that Rad52 is essential for maintaining genome stability in yeast cells with dysfunctional telomeres.
Our results suggest that Rad52 is required for maintaining telomeres in their normal state and preventing telomere shortening, which may be important for genome stability.

We found that wMelPop-infected mosquitoes were less likely to probe for blood, and when they did, they took longer to find it.
This study provides evidence of how Wolbachia infection affects mosquito physiology, which could be used to develop new strategies for controlling dengue transmission.

Circulating tumor cells ( CTCs) are cancer cells that circulate in the bloodstream and have been implicated as mediators of metastasis.

These findings suggest that circulating tumor cells are an important source of matrix metalloproteinases in metastatic breast cancer, which could serve as potential biomarkers to monitor disease progression and response to therapy.

The aim of this study was to evaluate the efficacy and safety of mesalazine in the treatment of active ulcerative colitis.

Quality of evidence The quality of evidence for each outcome is low or very low.
Key results We found no clear evidence on whether or not probiotics prevent recurrence of Crohn's disease.

There was little or no difference between probiotics and mesalazine for preventing relapse (low-certainty evidence), maintaining remission (low-certainty evidence), or improving quality of life (very-low certainty evidence).
We found that there is probably little or no difference between the combination of probiotics and 5-aminosalicylates and 5-aminosalicylates alone for maintaining remission.
The majority of the studies did not report any side effects from taking probiotics and there were few reports of side effects in those who took the placebo.

The aim of this review was to compare the effects of an invasive strategy with and without glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor antagonists (such as abciximab, tirofiban or eptifibatide) on death, myocardial infarction, stroke, bleeding complications and need for revascularization after percutaneous coronary intervention compared with a conservative strategy.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant studies published up to 25 August 2015.
The review authors found 10 studies with 11,000 participants that compared the two types of surgery.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant randomised controlled trials (RCTs) published up to May 2016, and found 13 RCTs with 2055 participants.
The review found that routine invasive management is associated with an increase in risk of bleeding complications and myocardial infarction when compared to selective invasive management.

The review found that there is some evidence that patients who had an invasive strategy were less likely to have a heart attack or require hospital admission for chest pain than those who had a selective invasive strategy.
The results showed that, when compared to an invasive strategy, a conservative strategy was associated with fewer deaths and less bleeding at one year.
Nucleosomes are fundamental units of chromatin, and their positions along DNA play an important role in regulating gene expression by controlling access of transcription factors to DNA.
Here, we use a combination of experimental and computational approaches to quantify the contribution of transcription factors ( TFs) to nucleosome positioning.

Our results suggest that the presence of nucleosomes in promoter regions is not necessarily an obstacle to transcription initiation, and that the absence of nucleosomes may be due to the action of specific regulatory factors.
Exposure therapy is an effective treatment for anxiety disorders; however, it is often difficult for patients to complete because of its aversive nature.
We show that it is possible to bypass this unpleasantness by using an approach that decodes neural activity associated with fear and pairs it with rewards.
This study shows how exposure to fear-relevant stimuli in an unconscious state can be used as a treatment for anxiety disorders.
Our results provide new insights into how plants sense light signals and respond to them to regulate shoot and root growth.
The N-methyl-d-aspartate receptor ( NMDA receptor) is a glutamate-gated ion channel that plays an important role in learning and memory.
We found that BK channels are expressed on the basal dendrites of a subset of cortical pyramidal cells and that they regulate excitatory synaptic transmission onto these dendrites.
The World Bank's Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper ( PRSP) is an important tool for governments to develop strategies to reduce poverty.
However, there is little evidence on how these data can be used to improve health care delivery and policy-making in low- and middle-income countries ( LMICs).
In this study, we use machine learning techniques to combine mobile phone data with environmental data to map multidimensional poverty across all 552 communes in Senegal.
Vaccines are an important tool for preventing infectious diseases, but their efficacy depends on the ability to elicit strong immune responses.
We show that Bl-Eng2 is an effective vaccine adjuvant in combination with a subunit vaccine targeting the fungal cell wall component beta-glucan.
This study provides evidence for the first time that targeting Dectin-2 with an engineered protein can enhance protective immune responses and reduce mortality in a mouse model of infection.
We show here that GHRH and D2R form heterodimers in vivo in the pituitary gland of mice.
Here, we show that the ghrelin receptor ( GHSR) and dopamine receptor 2 ( D2R) form a heteromeric complex with a stoichiometry of 1: 1.
Our results show that receptor heteromers are not only important for the regulation of G-protein signaling but also for the structural integrity and function of the receptor itself.

These findings provide new insights into the regulation of D2R signaling, which may be relevant for understanding the pathophysiology of neuropsychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease.
Here, we show that Runx1 is required for the development of mesenchyme-derived cells in vivo by generating two conditional knockout mouse models.
We found that Runx1 is required for the differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells to bone-forming osteoblasts, but not adipocytes.
Our results provide new insights into the function of Runx1 in bone formation and fat metabolism.

This work demonstrates the feasibility of using ^17O NMR for studying biological macromolecules and opens up new avenues for structural biology research.
The protozoan parasite Leishmania donovani causes visceral leishmaniasis ( VL), which is endemic in many parts of the world.
Here, we show that edelfosine induces cell death by targeting the mitochondrial respiratory chain complex V ( FOF1-ATP synthase) to lipid rafts.

Our results show that edelfosine is able to kill Leishmania parasites by targeting mitochondrial ATP production and disrupting membrane organization, which may be exploited as an alternative strategy for the treatment of this disease.
