We found that young flies with high levels of miR-1 are more likely to survive when exposed to stressors later in life.
We find that these two measures are highly correlated but do not always track each other perfectly, indicating that they reflect different aspects of aging.
We found that some miRNAs are expressed at different levels between long-lived and short-lived flies, and that these miRNA expression patterns are predictive of longevity.

We find that variation in insulin signaling during development is associated with adult longevity, suggesting that early life exposure to insulin-like peptides may be an important determinant of lifespan.
Amyloid fibril formation is a common feature of many neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease ( AD) and Parkinson's disease ( PD).
We have used solid-state NMR spectroscopy to determine the structure of amylin fibrils in solution at low resolution.

We show that the fibril growth rate depends on the concentration of the helical oligomer and not on the total protein concentration, indicating that the helical oligomer directly contributes to the elongation of the fibril.

We propose an elongation mechanism for insulin amyloid fibril formation in which the initial nucleating event involves the formation of a small oligomer with a defined structure, followed by growth through addition of monomers to the end of the growing fibril.
Our results suggest that blocking Sprouty proteins in CD8^+ T cells could be an effective strategy to enhance their function during chronic infections or cancer.
Epstein-Barr virus ( EBV) is a human herpesvirus associated with several cancers, including Burkitt's lymphoma and nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
Here, we show that EBNA1 interacts with RPL4 and that this interaction is required for EBV replication.
Our results suggest that EBV may use NCL to promote viral replication in infected cells, and that this process is dependent on the expression of the viral protein LMP1.
The bacterial enzyme Topoisomerase I ( TopoI) is essential for maintaining genome stability and is a target of antibiotics.
We found that transcriptional regulation by Topoisomerase I is mediated through direct interaction between the enzyme and RNA polymerase ( RNAP) at promoters.

We propose that TopoI is required to maintain the stability of the transcription machinery, possibly by preventing the formation of secondary structures within the nascent RNA or by maintaining the integrity of the DNA template.
Human polyomavirus BK ( BKV) is an opportunistic pathogen that infects most people worldwide but remains asymptomatic in healthy individuals.
Human polyomavirus BK ( BKPyV) is one of the most prevalent viruses in humans and has been linked to several diseases including nephropathy in kidney transplant recipients.
We describe here a novel strategy to identify new human polyomaviruses using a combination of molecular biology techniques and epidemiology.
We found that there are at least 20 new polyomavirus species infecting nonhuman primates.
We found that six of these viruses are new to science, and we were able to identify their closest relatives in humans.
We found that all four viruses were present in human sera collected in Cote d'Ivoire, but only two of them ( Hantaan virus and Seoul virus) were detected in German sera.
The p53 protein is a transcription factor that plays an important role in preventing cancer formation, but it also has many other functions.
We have used an approach called trans-intein splicing to label the N-terminal transactivation domain ( NTAD) of p53 with nitrogen-15 and observed its structure by solution NMR spectroscopy.
We show that this interaction is important for maintaining the specificity of p53 binding to its target genes and preventing nonspecific interactions with other DNA sequences.
The Rho family of small GTPases is involved in many cellular processes, including cell migration and division.

We found that the estrogen-related receptor alpha ( ERRa) transcription factor is required to maintain the integrity of the blood-testis barrier and that this function depends on its ability to regulate the expression of BACURD2, an adaptor protein that controls RhoA protein levels in Sertoli cells.
Our study shows that this regulation of RhoA activity is essential to ensure proper cell migration and tissue organization in vivo.

The relationship between plate tectonics and biodiversity has been debated for decades, with some arguing that plate tectonics is responsible for regulating biodiversity and others suggesting that it does not play an important role.
We show that the timing of major continental breakups has had a profound impact on the evolution of marine animals over the last 443 million years.
Our results show that the major geological events of the past 250 million years have had a profound impact on the evolution and diversification of marine animals.
The suprachiasmatic nucleus ( SCN) is the master circadian clock in mammals, and it synchronizes neuronal oscillations across the brain to maintain behavioral rhythms.
We find that disruption of intercellular communication between neurons leads to loss of rhythmic activity in most neurons, but not all.
We show that this mechanism is consistent with experimental data from single cells in culture and propose a new model for how circadian clocks are synchronized across tissues.
We find that coupled circadian oscillators can be synchronized to produce a coherent rhythm, but this requires a certain number of oscillators and a certain level of coupling strength.
The development of new drugs with high genetic barrier to resistance is an important goal in the fight against HIV/AIDS.
However, the optimal treatment strategy for these patients is still unknown.
Our results also highlight the importance of understanding the molecular mechanisms of action of antiviral drugs in order to predict how resistance will evolve.
The malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum undergoes a complex life cycle involving multiple developmental stages within two hosts.

We found that the number of proteins modified by ubiquitin increases dramatically during merozoite development from the preceding schizont stage, and we identify several novel targets for further study.
We found that inhibition of the ubiquitin activating enzyme ( UBA1), which is required for the addition of ubiquitin to proteins, was highly toxic to T. brucei.
We show that this compound is a potent inhibitor of Plasmodium falciparum UBA1 and identify key residues in the active site that are important for its activity.
Here we show that inhibition of the ubiquitin activating enzyme ( UBA1) blocks parasite growth and causes cell death.
We found that the drug targets UBA1, an enzyme involved in protein degradation, and that it blocks parasite development at the stage when parasites are preparing to divide.

We found that yeast cells expressing inducible forms of Ssa Hsp70 proteins were more resistant to a-synuclein toxicity than those expressing constitutively expressed counterparts.
Here, we report that the yeast Ssa3 protein, which is involved in mitochondrial function and protein quality control, promotes clearance of a-synuclein aggregates via autophagy.
The evolution of new functions in eukaryotic genomes is often associated with gene fusions, which are the result of chromosomal rearrangements between genes.
Here we present an analysis of the largest and most comprehensive dataset of eukaryote genomes to date.
This study provides new insights into the evolution of protein domains, which are the building blocks of proteins, and their role in the evolution of biological pathways.
Giardiasis, caused by Giardia lamblia, is a common intestinal infection worldwide.
Giardiasis is caused by the protozoan parasite Giardia lamblia, which infects humans and animals worldwide.
We have identified several kinases that are essential for parasite survival, and we show that some of them may be potential drug targets.
We found that two of these proteins, TgTSS1 and TgTSS2, are essential for parasite survival in vitro.
These results provide a foundation for the design and testing of novel anti-Giardia compounds.

The parathyroid hormone (PTH) receptor is an important target for treating diseases such as osteoporosis, but its activation by PTH has been difficult to achieve in vivo due to rapid degradation by proteases.
Here, we describe how we have used this approach to develop a new class of selective agonists for the G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) GPR56.
This work provides insight into how peptide structure affects receptor binding and suggests that backbone modifications could be used to improve the specificity of other GPCR ligands.
The Bcl-2 family proteins are central regulators of cell death in multicellular organisms.
We show here that BOK regulates the expression and activity of UMPS.
We found that BOK is highly expressed in normal human colon tissue but is downregulated in cancer cells and tumors resistant to 5-fluorouracil ( 5-FU).
We show that BOK is required to maintain normal levels of ATP and NADPH, which are important for cell survival and proliferation.
The immune response to filarial infection is characterized by a marked reduction in T cell responses and an increase in regulatory T cells ( Tregs).
We found that in MF patients there is an imbalance of immune response with decreased production of Th1 cytokines ( IFN-gamma) and increased levels of Th2 cytokine ( IL-13).
We found that the suppressive effect of MF on T cell responses was dependent on the presence of CD8+ T cells, but not CD4+ T cells.
This study provides new insights into the pathogenesis of lymphatic pathology in lymphatic filariasis, which could help to develop more effective treatments and prevent lymphedema.
Understanding how species diverge from one another is essential for understanding the origin of biodiversity.
However, little is known about how genetic variation underlying reproductive isolation evolves and accumulates over time.
We show that the genetic basis of this variation is largely determined by a single gene, which has undergone rapid evolution in response to selection from different pollinators.
We found that the two ecotypes have undergone strong selection for different alleles at this gene and that these alleles are associated with differences in the expression of the gene.
Our study provides insight into how natural selection acts on floral traits to promote reproductive isolation among closely related species.
We conducted a survey among 204 residents of an urban area in northern India to determine the factors influencing their knowledge, attitudes and practices regarding rabies and free-roaming dogs.
The results showed that there are significant knowledge gaps in the community about rabies, its symptoms, prevention and treatment.
The results showed that knowledge of rabies was higher among people who had higher education, income and age.
We conclude that there is an urgent need for public health education on rabies in this region, especially among low-income communities and school children.
We found that the community members' knowledge and awareness of rabies was low, but they were willing to participate in the campaign.

Neuroimaging studies have shown that humans' prosocial behavior is associated with activation of brain regions involved in cognitive control, suggesting that prosociality requires cognitive control over self-interest.
The question whether humans are fundamentally selfish or prosocial has been debated for decades.

We found that mice lacking both receptors had reduced parasite loads and less severe disease after infection with Leishmania major, but were not protected from infection with L. donovani.
However, how this rapid adaptation occurs remains unclear.

We found that transposable elements are responsible for a large proportion of genetic variation in flowering time and that they have been involved in the evolution of this trait in natural populations of Arabidopsis thaliana.
Electrochemical CO2 reduction is an emerging field with great potential for sustainable production of fuels and chemicals from CO2.
Copper is an attractive material for use in electrochemical applications due to its low cost, high abundance, and excellent electrical conductivity.
We report that copper nanoparticles ( NPs) enable efficient and selective production of C2-C3 hydrocarbons from CO2 reduction to formic acid and methanol.
G protein-coupled receptors ( GPCRs) are the largest family of membrane proteins in the human genome, with over 800 members.
The structural characterization of G protein-coupled receptors ( GPCRs) has been hampered by their inherent instability and heterogeneity.
Herein, we describe the design and synthesis of a series of potent and selective GPCR ligands that form stable covalent adducts with their cognate G proteins.
We have developed a new approach for crystallizing GPCRs by using a combination of a nanobody, which is a single-domain antibody fragment, and an agonist that forms a covalent bond with the receptor.
Alzheimer's disease ( AD) is the most common neurodegenerative disorder in the elderly population, and its etiology has not been fully understood.
BDNF is decreased in the brains of Alzheimer's disease ( AD) patients and mice with AD-like pathology.

We found that environmental stress activates C/EBPb, a proinflammatory transcription factor, to induce d-secretase expression in astrocytes, leading to increased A[beta]42 production and neurotoxicity.
Our results suggest that the combination of TBI, high-fat diet, and chronic cold stress may be a major contributor to the development of AD pathology.

We propose a computational model of how this process works and show that it is able to learn complex sequences with high accuracy.
We show how this process is implemented by a network of neurons with different temporal properties.
We show that this model reproduces many aspects of human behavior, including the distribution of chunk sizes and durations, and the pauses between them.
We show that the brain's cortical structure is not random, but rather follows a hierarchical organization.
We find that these fractions are remarkably consistent across different species of mammals, suggesting that they may play an important role in brain function.
We propose a new principle for cortical organization, namely "spine economy maximization," which is based on maximizing the number of spines per unit area of cortex.
We find that the model with the best fit to the experimental data has a large number of small spines, but a smaller number of larger spines than previously thought.

Our findings provide evidence that the efficiency of neural circuits is determined by the total volume of wires rather than their lengths or temporal delays, suggesting that the new spine economy principle may play an important role in the evolution of the brain.
Immunonutrition for people with burns Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether giving nutritional supplements to people who have suffered a serious burn injury improves their recovery.
The review authors found no evidence for benefit from adding extra nutrients to the standard diet for people with burns.
We did not find enough evidence to be sure whether giving glutamine is helpful for people with cancer.
The use of penoxsulam ( PS) as an herbicide has been increasing rapidly due to its high efficacy and low toxicity to humans and animals.
The mechanism by which it inhibits the enzyme has not been reported.
We have determined the crystal structure of the protein-protein interaction between the human p53 tumor suppressor and the viral protein Mdm2, which is responsible for its degradation.
We have determined the structure of the complex formed between the herbicide pyridylsulfone ( PS) and the target enzyme acetyl-CoA carboxylase ( AHAS).
The brain is constantly exposed to a stream of sensory stimuli, and it must be able to extract information from this stream in order to make sense of the world around us.
The study of these correlations has led to a better understanding of how neurons encode information about sensory stimuli.
Theoretical models of neural networks have been proposed to explain this phenomenon, but they are often based on simplifying assumptions about how neurons interact with each other.
We use computational modeling to explore how different circuit architectures affect the relationship between stimulus and correlation.
We show that this is possible by using a simple recurrent neural network model, which we fit to experimental data from the visual cortex of awake mice.
We also show that the ability to discriminate between stimuli is highly dependent on the strength of the connections within the network.
Haemophilus influenzae type b ( Hib) is a bacterium that causes serious infections in people with sickle cell disease.
We wanted to find out if giving these vaccines could prevent infections in people with sickle cell disease.

We also looked for differences between different types of Hib vaccine, how well they work when given alone versus when combined with other vaccines, and whether there are differences in response based on age at first vaccination.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant studies up to 18 December 2017.
We did not find any studies on the use of Hib vaccine in people with sickle cell disease.
We found that the use of anti-VEGF therapy in combination with laser treatment was more effective than laser alone for treating macular oedema due to diabetic retinopathy.

The aim of this study was to compare the clinical effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of ranibizumab ( Lucentis( r), Novartis Pharma UK Ltd, Frimley, UK) and bevacizumab ( Avastin( r), F. Hoffmann-La Roche AG, Basel, Switzerland) in treating macular oedema secondary to CRVO.

The aim of this study was to find out which treatment is best for people with macular oedema after a central retinal vein blockage, by comparing the effects of ranibizumab ( Lucentis), bevacizumab ( Avastin) and aflibercept ( Eylea).
We found no evidence of benefit from using bevacizumab compared with ranibizumab for treating wet AMD, but we were not able to exclude the possibility that it might have some effect.
We found that there was not enough evidence to say whether or not this treatment works well for people with cancer pain.
The ability of natural populations to respond to environmental challenges is determined by their genetic makeup.
The ability to metabolize toxic compounds is an important factor in determining susceptibility to toxicity.
We have developed an approach for identifying genetic variants that affect the response of Drosophila melanogaster to toxic chemicals.
We found that genetic variation in CYP1A2, which is known to be involved in caffeine metabolism, accounts for ~20% of the variance in caffeine sensitivity.

We identified four quantitative trait loci ( QTL) for resistance to this toxin and found that they contain genes from the cytochrome P450 family, which are known to be involved in insecticide metabolism.
We show that the gene Cyp12d1 is associated with caffeine resistance in Drosophila melanogaster and that this association is causal.
However, most of these compounds do not have the desired biological activity.

Copper is an essential element for many biological processes, including those involved in cancer cell proliferation and survival.
We show that the liver cancer alters the metabolism of both copper and sulfur, leading to an imbalance between these two elements.

We found that the blood of HCC patients contains elevated levels of sulfur, which is consistent with the idea that some of this sulfur comes from the tumor itself.
Florigen is a plant hormone that promotes flowering in leaves and suppresses shoot elongation in stems.

The transition from vegetative to reproductive development in Arabidopsis thaliana is accompanied by a reorganization of shoot architecture, which involves changes in cell division patterns and cell expansion rates.
This study reveals how a single transcription factor, florigen, coordinates two independent developmental processes in plants.
Infection with fishborne zoonotic trematodes ( FZT) is a major public health problem in Southeast Asia, where it causes significant morbidity and mortality.
In order to control schistosomiasis, WHO recommends regular mass drug administration (MDA) with praziquantel to all school-aged children and adults living in endemic areas.

In this study, we evaluated the effect of preventive chemotherapy on the prevalence and intensity of infection with hookworms in school-aged children living in a rural community in northern Vietnam where hookworm is highly prevalent.
The main control strategy is mass drug administration ( MDA) of praziquantel in school-aged children and adults.

Leishmaniasis is a disease caused by Leishmania parasites and transmitted by sandflies.

We have developed an inactivated whole cell vaccine against rabies virus ( RABV) using a novel approach based on the use of a non-pathogenic canine distemper virus ( CDV) as a vector for the expression of the RABV glycoprotein G.
The current study describes the development of a new vaccine candidate for human use against rabies.

The enzyme promiscuity is an important feature of enzymes, which allows them to catalyze reactions with different substrates and thus provides the flexibility for adaptation to new environments.
We have developed a computational method to identify potential promiscuous enzymes in metabolic networks and apply it to the E. coli metabolic model.
Here, we present a workflow that combines computational methods with experimental approaches to predict metabolic promiscuity in E. coli.
Our results demonstrate how a systematic approach to metabolic modeling can be used to identify new biochemical reactions and pathways, and provide insight into the regulation of these processes.
Flow cytometry is an important tool for studying biological systems, but it is limited by its ability to measure only a few parameters at a time.
Single-cell technologies have become increasingly important in many areas of biology and medicine, but they are often difficult to analyze due to their high dimensionality.
CytoPy is an open-source software package that provides a flexible and user-friendly interface to analyze flow cytometry data in a reproducible manner.
CytoPy is able to identify rare immune cells, including regulatory T cells, natural killer cells, and memory B cells, with high accuracy.
We applied this method to study the immune response to bacterial infections, and found that it is possible to identify specific cell types associated with disease progression.
The role of gut microbiota in host adaptation to cold temperatures has not been well studied.

We found that the gut microbiota of B. dorsalis is essential for its survival at low temperatures, and that it promotes the host's ability to resist cold stress through enhancing the utilization of amino acids such as arginine and proline.
Autoimmune diseases such as multiple sclerosis ( MS) are characterized by an abnormal immune response to self-antigens, which results in damage to the central nervous system ( CNS).

We found that ADAM9 is highly expressed in human CD4^+ T cells and that its expression is elevated in patients with autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis ( RA) and systemic lupus erythematosus ( SLE).
We found that ADAM9 is required for TGF-b activation by Th17 cells and promotes their differentiation.
People with COPD have varying levels of breathlessness, exercise capacity and quality of life.
The aim of this review was to find out if multidisciplinary programs are effective in improving quality of life, exercise capacity and lung function in people with COPD.
We wanted to find out if these programs could improve quality of life, exercise capacity and reduce hospital admissions for flare-ups.
We found 10 studies of 11,000 participants in total that evaluated the effects of an integrated disease management program for patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).
We found 26 studies with 2997 participants who had been diagnosed with depression and were randomised to receive either cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) or treatment as usual (TAU).
We found that people with dementia are often treated in different ways, depending on where they live.
The results showed that participants who received an exercise program improved their six-minute walking test by 44 meters more than those who did not receive an exercise program.
The intervention reduced the number of people who had at least one admission to hospital by 13%.
The review of trials found no evidence that either treatment is better than the other in terms of reducing death, improving lung function or quality of life.

The use of chimeric antigen receptors ( CARs) to redirect T cells against cancer has shown great promise in clinical trials, but it remains unclear how to improve their efficacy and safety.
The use of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells to treat cancer has shown promise in hematological malignancies but has been less successful in solid tumors.
However, it remains challenging to track the fate and function of CAR T cells in vivo.
We have developed an MRI-compatible method to label CAR T cells with iron oxide nanoparticles.
We demonstrate that this approach is feasible and provides a means to monitor CAR T cell persistence in vivo.
Kidney stones are a common problem in the United States, affecting approximately 10% of men and 5% of women at some point in their lives.
We performed a genome-wide association study to identify genetic variants associated with kidney stone formation in a large cohort of Japanese individuals.

Our results show that, in contrast to previous studies, India's renewable energy potential is not sufficient to displace existing coal-fired capacity by 2030, but it could reduce emissions from the sector by more than half.
We find that adding 600 GW of wind power to India's grid would reduce emissions from electricity generation by 1.

The brain's ability to learn and remember new information depends on changes in the strength of connections between neurons, known as synaptic plasticity.
We show how this mechanism could be implemented in the brain, and we demonstrate its ability to learn optimal behavior in a simulated environment.
Multiple sclerosis ( MS) is an autoimmune disease of the central nervous system characterized by demyelination, axonal loss and neurodegeneration.

We found that CRYAB expression was upregulated in the central nervous system ( CNS) during experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis and in the cuprizone mouse model of demyelination.

The use of simple behavioural interventions is common in the treatment of bedwetting.
We found 16 studies with 1643 children who had received a behaviour therapy or control treatment.

However, there was not enough evidence to support the use of simple behavioural treatments alone for this condition.

Our results suggest that EVs may be an important mediator of immune tolerance in pregnancy and other settings where microchimerism is present.

R is widely used in many fields, including data science, statistics, machine learning, artificial intelligence, scientific computing, and more.

We propose a multiscale computational model for the simulation of the intracellular transport of vesicles in the cytoplasm of cells.
Listeria monocytogenes is an opportunistic pathogen that causes foodborne illness in humans and animals.
In this study we show that Listeria monocytogenes interacts with the major vault protein ( MVP) which is the main component of cytoplasmic ribonucleoprotein particles called vaults.
These findings provide new insights into how bacteria interact with host cells and may help us better understand the function of vaults in human cells.

This study also shows how the codon usage in mitochondria is related to the function and expression level of proteins encoded by these genes, which may be important for understanding the pathogenesis of diseases caused by mutations in mitochondrial translation factors.
Bacteriophage ( phage) therapy has been proposed as an alternative treatment option for bacterial infections, but its efficacy and safety are still unknown.
Phages are viruses that infect bacteria and have been proposed as a treatment for bacterial infections due to their ability to target specific bacteria with high specificity and efficacy.
We have developed a method for making phage-based antimicrobials that are self-destructive.
We show that this method is capable of selectively killing bacteria in complex biological systems, such as the human gut microbiome.
This work demonstrates an efficient and targeted approach to kill bacteria using phage-based nanotechnology.
Centronuclear myopathies ( CNMs) are a group of rare, slowly progressing skeletal muscle disorders characterized by abnormal positioning of nuclei within muscle fibers.
We have identified a novel mutation in the gene encoding Bin1, which is expressed specifically in skeletal muscle, in a family with a severe form of centronuclear myopathy.
This study provides a new animal model to study the pathogenesis of CNM, which will be useful in developing treatments for this disease.

We show that this is due to reduced hypoxia-inducible factor-2 alpha ( HIF2A) expression, which is required for angiogenesis and tumor growth.

We used an experimental approach called array comparative genomic hybridization ( array CGH) to study the genetic changes found in zebrafish ERMS.
We found that these same regions are also frequently amplified in human cancer, suggesting that they may be important for tumor growth and survival.

Stochastic simulation is often used for modeling biological systems, but it can be computationally expensive and difficult to interpret.
We present a new method to simulate large-scale spatiotemporal models of biological systems and demonstrate its application in modeling the dynamics of a simple model of the cell cycle.
We apply our method to a stochastic model of cell polarization, and show how it can be used to identify the parameters that are most important for the robustness of this process.

Autophagy is a cellular process by which cells recycle damaged organelles and proteins to maintain homeostasis.
Here, we show that HER2 promotes autophagy in breast cancer cells and that inhibition of autophagy potentiates the antitumor activity of trastuzumab ( Herceptin) against HER2-positive breast cancers.
We found that autophagy is required for HER2-induced breast cancer progression by promoting cell proliferation and survival of tumor-initiating cells.
These results suggest that targeting the interaction between HER2 and Beclin 1 or increasing autophagy could be a novel strategy to treat HER2-positive breast cancer.
Here, we describe a microfluidic device that enables the generation of an array of nanoliter droplets in a single operation.
We demonstrate that this platform is capable of generating microfluidic droplet arrays, which are confined to a nanowell and in direct contact with a solid surface.
We demonstrate that this device can be used to deliver high concentrations of oxygen in a controlled manner, and we show that it has potential as an alternative to traditional ventilation systems.
Artificial photosynthesis is an emerging field of research focused on mimicking nature's light-driven water oxidation reaction to produce chemical fuels and chemicals.
However, the use of molecular catalysts for water oxidation is still limited due to their low activity and poor durability.

The seismic properties of the outer core are difficult to measure because it is inaccessible to direct sampling and observation.
We show that the Earth's outer core is likely composed of an Fe-Ni-Si-O alloy system and demonstrate that this composition is incompatible with a homogeneous solid inner core.
Here, we show that iron silicate liquids are immiscible with iron-rich melts in the Earth's lower mantle by combining high-pressure experiments and computer simulations.
The existence of such an immiscible phase has important implications for understanding the structure and evolution of the Earth's interior.
The ability of animals to produce complex sounds is essential for their survival, but how this ability evolved remains unknown.

The myosin motor protein is one of the most important proteins in cells, powering many essential biological processes such as muscle contraction and cell migration.
The molecular mechanisms of how myosin senses mechanical load are not well understood.
We have found that the N-terminal region of myosin-I is important for regulating its mechanical properties.
The endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) is an organelle responsible for protein synthesis in eukaryotic cells.
The endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) is an organelle found in eukaryotic cells that is responsible for protein synthesis and folding.

We found that the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) is the site for translation initiation and that the ER membrane contains a number of proteins that are required to initiate translation.
We show that this protein is required for the localization of many mRNAs to the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) and that it acts by binding to specific sequences within these mRNAs.
Sexual behavior is an important aspect of human social interaction, but the underlying mechanisms are not well understood.

Our results challenge the idea that the brain's response to visual sexual stimuli is sexually dimorphic, suggesting that the neural mechanisms underlying human sexual arousal may be more similar than previously thought.
We found no evidence for a link between structural features of the brain and its response to sexual stimuli.

The tyrannosaurs were among the most successful terrestrial predators of all time, but their fossil record is biased toward juveniles and subadults.

Dendritic cells ( DCs) play an essential role in initiating immune responses by capturing pathogens and presenting their components to T cells.

Here, we demonstrate that DCs and macrophages are able to kill Legionella bacteria in an autophagy-independent manner via a caspase-1-dependent mechanism.
We show that apoptosis is induced in dendritic cells ( DCs) but not in macrophages, and that this is due to the ability of Legionella to induce activation of caspase-3.
We also show that the host cell death pathway is activated by Legionella infection, but that this does not contribute to bacterial clearance.
We found that DCs have an intrinsic mechanism for limiting bacterial growth by activating apoptosis in response to infection with Salmonella.

This study provides new insights into the behavior, mobility, and social organization of Neandertals in Europe during the Middle Paleolithic period.
The genome is organized in three-dimensional space within the nucleus, with genomic regions of similar transcriptional activity clustering together to form chromatin domains.
We have used the nematode C. elegans to study how genes are organized within the nucleus, and how this affects their expression.

We found that loss of histone methylation leads to defects in compartmentalization, which is consistent with the idea that chromatin compaction is important for maintaining the integrity of these domains.

We find that genes that switch compartments have similar expression patterns to those that do not change compartments, indicating that compartmentalization is not directly related to gene expression level.

Rift Valley fever is an emerging viral disease of livestock and humans caused by Rift Valley fever virus ( RVFV).
The virus is transmitted by mosquitoes and ticks, and it has been reported to cause severe disease in humans including fever, hemorrhagic fever, and encephalitis.

In this study, we used an animal model to investigate the pathogenesis of RVFV-induced encephalitis and found that intranasal inoculation is the most efficient route for inducing severe neurological symptoms.
We found that in infected rats, the brain is invaded by large numbers of immune cells including neutrophils, macrophages, and microglial cells.

Neurotransmitter release is a fundamental process in neuronal communication, but its underlying mechanisms are still not fully understood.

However, it remains unclear how synaptic depression affects the information transmission in neural networks.

There is currently no vaccine available to prevent infection with Leishmania parasites and treatment of disease is often difficult due to drug resistance.
In this study we show that TLR9 is important for controlling Leishmania amazonensis infection in mice.

These findings provide new insights into the role of TLR9 in host defense against Leishmania infection and highlight the potential use of TLR9 agonists as adjuvants for intranasal vaccines against cutaneous leishmaniasis.

The cohesin complex is an essential protein complex that plays critical roles in chromosome segregation during cell division.

This study provides new insights into how oxygen may have affected the evolution of life on Earth.

Inhaled bronchodilators for cystic fibrosis Review question We reviewed the evidence on how well different types of inhalers work compared with each other, or when combined with nebulisers (a device which turns liquid medication into an aerosol), to open up airways in people with cystic fibrosis.
Nebulisers are devices used to deliver medication directly into the lungs via an aerosol mist.
The review authors found 10 studies, with a total of 1115 children aged from one month to 18 years who had received either a metered-dose inhaler or a nebuliser.
We found six studies that compared different types of nebuliser system in children aged under 18 years with cystic fibrosis.
We found no studies that compared the two types of surgery together, so we could not combine their results.
The review found that using a high flow nebuliser system resulted in better delivery of medication to the lungs and was more convenient for people with cystic fibrosis than low flow nebulisers.
The use of nebuliser devices with vibrating mesh technology may be more effective than other types of nebuliser for delivering bronchodilators and corticosteroids, but this is not clear.
We found that there are many different types of inhaler available and that they all have their own advantages and disadvantages.

The domestic dog is an important model organism in biomedical research, but its genetic diversity has been understudied compared to other species such as humans or mice.

Our results suggest that targeting Vpu may be an effective strategy to reduce viral reservoirs in HIV-infected patients.

Mucosal-associated invariant T ( MAIT) cells are innate-like T lymphocytes that recognize microbial products presented by MHC class I-related molecules.

We found that the functionally impaired MAIT cells were characterized by an aberrant expression of transcription factors, which was associated with their defective effector functions.

Low molecular weight heparin versus unfractionated heparin in the initial treatment of venous thromboembolism Review question We reviewed the evidence comparing the use of low molecular weight heparin with unfractionated heparin as an initial treatment for people with venous thromboembolism.

We found one study that compared fixed dose low molecular weight heparin to adjusted dose unfractionated heparin and two studies that compared fixed dose low molecular weight heparin to adjusted dose intravenous unfractionated heparin.
The risk of bleeding complications was similar between patients receiving low molecular weight heparin and unfractionated heparin.
We found that using a fixed dose of LMWH was better than using UFH in terms of reducing the risk of recurrence of blood clots, and also reducing bleeding complications.
Quality of the evidence The overall quality of the evidence for each outcome is low, as there were only two studies included in this review and these had some methodological issues.

Cognitive-behavioural therapy for major depressive disorder (CBT) Background Depression is a common mental health problem affecting millions of people worldwide.

The symptoms are usually severe enough to interfere with daily functioning and include changes in appetite, sleep disturbances, fatigue, concentration problems, lack of interest in activities, and thoughts of death or suicide.
The current treatment options are not ideal, as they have many side effects and do not work well in all people with depression.
It has been suggested that agomelatine, an atypical antidepressant drug with melatonin receptor agonist properties, may be effective in treating depression.
Agomelatine is an antidepressant drug that has been used for treating depression since 2008.
The review found no evidence of difference in efficacy between agomelatine and any of the other antidepressant drugs included in this review.
Agomelatine was not significantly different from any of these drugs in terms of efficacy, with no clear evidence that it is better than any of them.
Agomelatine was also more well-tolerated than venlafaxine with fewer people discontinuing treatment due to adverse events.

The results showed that agomelatine was no more effective than placebo or other antidepressants in treating depressive disorders, and it had similar rates of adverse events as compared with placebo or other antidepressants.
The review authors searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing agomelatine with any of the other drugs used to treat depression.

We found that the intervention was successful at getting more children to collect their asthma medication and have scheduled contact with health-care professionals but had no effect on reducing unplanned visits or admissions.

The use of nasal intermittent positive-pressure ventilation versus nasal continuous positive airway pressure for treatment of preterm infants with respiratory distress syndrome Review question We reviewed the evidence on the effectiveness and safety of nasal intermittent positive-pressure ventilation (NIPPV) compared to nasal continuous positive airway pressure (NCPAP) for the treatment of breathing difficulties in preterm babies.

Heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula for preterm infants Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether or not to use a heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula (HHHFNC) for preterm babies who are receiving respiratory support with oxygen therapy.
We found one study that compared high-flow nasal cannula to usual care in babies who had been ventilated.
We found no evidence to support the use of high-frequency nasal cannulae for preterm infants in the neonatal unit, and there was some concern about their safety.

Here, we show that liver-resident MAIT and NK cells are the major producers of IFN-g in response to ssRNA stimulation.
We found that liver-resident NK cells are activated and produce IFN-g in response to HBV or HCV infection.

We searched for all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) published up to July 2015 and found 18 studies with 1252 participants.
The results of our review are limited by the fact that all trials were conducted in different ways, using different methods for measuring the outcomes.
We recommend that a large-scale randomised controlled trial be conducted to confirm whether or not this treatment works.

The length of time spent in hospital is an important factor to consider when evaluating the quality of care provided by psychiatric hospitals.
The length of time spent in hospital is often used as an indicator of quality of care but it has been suggested that shorter lengths of stay are better for patients than longer ones.

Short stay or brief admission to hospital for people with serious mental health problems Review question We wanted to find out if short stay or brief admissions to hospital are better than standard long stay admissions for people with serious mental health problems.
Study characteristics The review authors searched for all relevant randomised controlled trials (RCTs) published up to May 2012.

We found 10 studies, involving 1237 participants, which compared brief (less than one week) hospital stays with standard (over one week) hospital stays for people with schizophrenia or other serious mental illness.
We found no significant differences between groups in terms of number of people who dropped out of treatment or were not able to be contacted for follow-up.

There were no differences in relapse rates between long and short stay, but people who had shorter stays did have better social functioning, including being more likely to be employed, able to housekeep, or have an unknown employment status.
However, more research is needed to determine whether or not this approach should be adopted as standard practice in all hospitals.
